text: Proportional access to professions or The middle way between free competition and commercial farmage. This would be a debate of the greatest importance if civilisation were to continue even for another ten years. But as that misfortune is unlikely to happen, it will be enough merely to touch on the question, and show that commerce is in danger of being subjected to farmage because of the need to remedy its increasing anarchy.
author: Charles Fourier
source: The Theory of the Four Movements
imageCaption: Crops Kansas, Public Domain, Wikimedia Commons

didItHoldUp.analysis: Fourier believed civilization itself had perhaps ten years left — that unchecked commercial competition would collapse into anarchy. Two centuries later, civilization persists, and commerce has only accelerated. But his underlying anxiety isn't entirely wrong: the tension between free markets and regulated access to professions remains one of the defining debates in economics. Gig economy, licensing battles, antitrust regulation — these are modern versions of Fourier's 'middle way.' He was catastrophically wrong about the timeline but planted a seed of critique that still flowers in every debate about whether markets need guardrails.

text: The hordes are making daily inroads into the cultivated areas of Asia, spilling further and further beyond their natural boundary, the Himalayan mountain range that stretches from Bokhara to China. At our very gates, the hordes are surging all over Turkey. Fifty more years of persecution from the Ottoman Empire and we shall see the whole of that fine empire returned to the life of nomads or Tartars, who are making terrifying progress against Turkish domination.
author: Charles Fourier
source: The Theory of the Four Movements
imageCaption: André Salles, en avant de la porte de Langson, Public Domain, Wikimedia Commons

didItHoldUp.analysis: Fourier's geopolitical vision was garbled — the Himalayas don't stretch from Bukhara to China, and the 'hordes' framing reveals deep European anxieties about Central Asian peoples. But his prediction about the Ottoman Empire was directionally correct: by 1858, the Ottoman state was in serious decline (the 'Sick Man of Europe'), and by 1922 it had collapsed entirely. The empire didn't return to 'nomadic life,' but it did fragment into dozens of successor states. Fourier's apocalyptic tone was wrong, but his instinct that Ottoman power was crumbling was sound — he just wrapped a reasonable observation in catastrophist fantasy.

text: The Americans have need of the telephone, but we do not. We have plenty of messenger boys.
author: Sir William Preece
source: Attributed, parliamentary testimony, 1876

didItHoldUp.analysis: Britain's Chief Engineer of the Post Office essentially argued that running children through the streets was a superior communication system to electrical voice transmission. Britain adopted the telephone within years, and Preece himself later became a champion of wireless telegraphy. The quote captures a timeless pattern: incumbents in communication systems consistently underestimate the next technology, because they can't imagine why anyone would want something they've never experienced.

text: When the Paris Exhibition closes, electric light will close with it and no more will be heard of it.
author: Erasmus Wilson
source: Attributed, c. 1878

didItHoldUp.analysis: Edison's practical incandescent bulb arrived just one year later in 1879. Within two decades, electric lighting was transforming cities worldwide. Today, artificial light is so ubiquitous that light pollution is a genuine environmental concern — we have so much electric light that it's visible from space. Wilson saw a demonstration and assumed it was a party trick. It turned out to be the end of darkness itself.

text: At present weapons are being changed every ten years, and even more frequently. In another fifteen years or so, people will use for shooting not rifles but some kind of lightning, some sort of a machine emitting a holocaustal electrical stream. Tell me: what can we invent in this line so as to surprise our neighbors? What if in fifteen years every great power will have secretly stored away one such surprise for any kind of eventuality? Alas, we are merely capable of imitating and purchasing ammunition from others, and—at best—of repairing it at home. To invent such machines we should have to have our own independent, and not purchased, science, not an imported but a free one—one that has taken root in our soil. As yet we do not possess such a science, nor do we even have a purchased one.
author: Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky
source: The Diary Of A Writer

didItHoldUp.analysis: Dostoevsky — a novelist, not a scientist — predicted directed energy weapons and the secret arms race 70 years before the Manhattan Project. His 'holocaustal electrical stream' isn't far from a description of nuclear weapons or, more precisely, modern directed energy weapons. His deeper insight was about the arms race itself: great powers secretly stockpiling decisive weapons, and Russia's dependence on imported science. That geopolitical anxiety proved prophetic throughout the 20th century. The novelist saw the pattern that the generals missed: the next war would be won not by soldiers, but by scientists.

text: The power of population is indefinitely greater than the power in the earth to produce subsistence for man. Population, when unchecked, increases in a geometrical ratio. Subsistence increases only in an arithmetical ratio.
author: Thomas Robert Malthus
source: An Essay on the Principle of Population, J. Johnson, London, 1798, Chapter 1
annotation: Malthus published his Essay just as the Industrial Revolution was about to prove him wrong for two centuries. Agricultural productivity increased geometrically, not arithmetically, through mechanization, fertilizers, and crop science. Yet Malthusian logic recurs in every generation's environmental anxiety, from Ehrlich to the Limits to Growth to modern degrowth movements.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Global population grew from ~1 billion in 1800 to 8 billion by 2022, while calories per capita increased substantially. Famines became rarer and were driven by politics, not absolute scarcity.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from An Essay on the Principle of Population (1798), Chapter 1: 'The power of population is indefinitely greater than the power in the earth to produce subsistence for man.'

text: I believe, in the future, wires will unite the head offices of the Telephone Company in different cities, and a man in one part of the country may communicate by word of mouth with another in a distant place.
author: Alexander Graham Bell
source: Letter to potential investors, March 25, 1878. Reproduced in Robert V. Bruce, Bell: Alexander Graham Bell and the Conquest of Solitude, Little Brown, 1973
annotation: Bell's prediction, made just two years after patenting the telephone, was modest compared to what actually transpired. He imagined it connecting offices; it would connect every home. His defensiveness — 'such ideas may appear Utopian' — reveals how radical even this limited vision seemed. Long-distance telephony was achieved by 1892 (New York to Chicago).

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 1900, there were over 600,000 telephones in the US. By 2000, there were over 1 billion phone lines worldwide. Mobile phones would add billions more.

verificationNote: Bell did write about telephone networks. A documented version from his 1877 London prospectus reads: 'I believe, in the future, wires will unite the head offices of the Telephone Company in different cities, and a man in one part of the country may communicate by word of mouth with another in a distant place.' Our entry begins similarly but may abbreviate. Source is 1877, not 1876 as the entry ID implies.

text: The horse is here to stay but the automobile is only a novelty — a fad.
author: Horace Rackham's banker
source: Advice given to Rackham regarding Ford Motor Company investment, 1903

didItHoldUp.analysis: Rackham ignored his banker and invested $5,000 in the Ford Motor Company. That stake eventually became worth $12.5 million. By 1920, horses were vanishing from American streets. By 1950, the automobile had reshaped every city in America, created suburbs, and defined an entire culture. The banker's prediction perfectly captures the difficulty of seeing past the dominant paradigm — and the cost of that failure. Sometimes the most expensive advice is the conventional wisdom.

text: The next war must be resolved in a single great battle, the decision secured by a massive enveloping movement. The campaign must be brought to a decisive conclusion within six weeks.
author: Alfred von Schlieffen
source: Memorandum on the war against France, December 1905. Published in Gerhard Ritter, The Schlieffen Plan: Critique of a Myth, Oswald Wolff, 1958
annotation: Schlieffen's plan for a rapid western campaign became the template for Germany's 1914 invasion of France. The six-week timeline was based on the assumption that France would collapse as in 1870. Instead, the campaign stalled at the Marne, producing four years of trench warfare. The plan's failure is the foundational case study in the gap between military planning and wartime reality.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The modified Schlieffen Plan was executed in August 1914. The German advance was halted at the Battle of the Marne (September 1914). The Western Front became a 4-year stalemate costing millions of lives.

verificationNote: Schlieffen's strategic doctrine is well documented, but this specific English-language quote could not be verified to a primary source. It may be a paraphrase of his strategic writings.

text: The Great War had been the explosion of a vast accumulation of energy, moral and social as well as material. Europe might, and probably would, bicker, murder, bomb, massacre, and starve, but for another generation at least she would not have either the spirit or the discipline or the material to produce such munitions and such wide-sweeping concerted action as devastated her in the Great War. She is morally and physically bankrupt and prostrate. She may go on sinking, as Asia Minor sank, back even to barbarism. Even if she does not do so, it will take forty or fifty years to reassemble energy for another such world-wide outbreak.
author: H G Wells
source: A Year of Prophesying

didItHoldUp.analysis: Wells gave Europe at least a generation before it could wage another world war. He was off by exactly one generation — World War II erupted just 15 years later, not 40 or 50. His reasoning was sound: Europe was devastated, bankrupt, and exhausted after WWI. But he underestimated how quickly industrial nations can rearm when driven by ideology, grievance, and totalitarian mobilization. Germany went from prostrate to the most powerful military in Europe in under a decade. Wells saw the exhaustion clearly but missed the fury that would fuel the recovery. The 'spirit and discipline' he thought were spent turned out to be renewable resources.

text: [...] we have been expressly evolved by nature-with all our impulses and deepest instincts-for the purpose of solving the economic problem. If the economic problem is solved, mankind will be deprived of its traditional purpose. Will this be a benefit? If one believes at all in the real values of life, the prospect at least opens up the possibility of benefit. Yet I think with dread of the readjustment of the habits and instincts of the ordinary man, bred into him for countless generations, which he may be asked to discard within a few decades.
author: John Maynard Keynes
source: Economic Possibilities for our Grandchildren

didItHoldUp.analysis: Keynes asked the deepest question in economics: what happens when we solve scarcity? His dread was prophetic. In wealthy nations, the economic problem is largely solved — most people have food, shelter, and material comfort their grandparents couldn't imagine. But the existential crisis Keynes feared has arrived differently than expected. Instead of leisure and philosophy, we got consumerism and anxiety. The 'readjustment' he worried about shows up as epidemic depression, addiction, and a crisis of meaning in the world's richest societies. We solved the economic problem. We didn't solve the human one.

text: The coming of the wireless era will make war impossible, because it will make war ridiculous.
author: Guglielmo Marconi
source: Technical World Magazine, 1912
annotation: Marconi's claim that communication technology would prevent war was published less than two years before World War I. The belief that connectivity produces peace has been repeatedly asserted and repeatedly disproven — for the telegraph, radio, television, and the internet. Each generation's communication utopians make essentially the same claim.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Two years later, World War I erupted — and radio was used extensively for military coordination. Every subsequent communication breakthrough has inspired the same hope: television will show us the horrors of war, the internet will connect us across borders, social media will foster understanding. Every time, the technology gets weaponized instead. Marconi's optimism is touching and persistent, a dream that each generation reinvents with the latest tool and that reality keeps dashing.

verificationNote: Exact quote confirmed: 'The coming of the wireless era will make war impossible, because it will make war ridiculous.' Sourced to Technical World Magazine, October 1912, p. 145, article titled 'Marconi's Plans for the World.'

text: So, one after another, either as pilots or passengers, will the members of the club ascend; and before the sheds are closed and the aerodrome deserted, each and all will have soared in flight, and tasted that thrill and exultation which comes of a rush in a plane through the cool of the evening air. But today, if we try to grasp such a notion as this, our state of mind is very like that of our grandfathers had some prophet dared tell them the day would dawn when, seated comfortably at dinner in a car on wheels, men would be drawn by an engine at 60 miles an hour: and yet the man or woman who has not, say twenty years hence, made a journey through the air, will be in exactly the same position as one who, at the present time, has never been by train.
author: Claude Grahame-White
source: The Aeroplane

didItHoldUp.analysis: Grahame-White, writing just 11 years after the Wright brothers' first flight, predicted that within 20 years air travel would be as normal as rail travel. By 1934, commercial aviation was growing rapidly — Imperial Airways, Pan Am, and Lufthansa were connecting continents. By the jet age of the 1960s, his vision was fully realized. What makes this passage beautiful is the rhetorical move: he compares the incredulity of his readers to that of their grandparents facing trains. Each generation struggles to believe in the next revolution, even as they live comfortably with the last one. The pattern holds today — replace 'aeroplane' with 'autonomous vehicle' and the sentiment is identical.

text: I really believe that the airplane will help peace in more ways than one. I think it will have a tendency to make war impossible.
author: Orville Wright
source: Interview in the Chicago Herald, 1917. Quoted in Fred Howard, Wilbur and Orville: A Biography of the Wright Brothers, Knopf, 1987, p. 402
annotation: Wright made this claim while the airplane was already being used as a weapon in World War I. His logic — that aerial observation would make surprise impossible, and thus war futile — echoed similar claims made about every military technology from the crossbow to the nuclear bomb. The strategic bombing campaigns of World War II provided the definitive refutation.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Aviation became a devastating military weapon. Strategic bombing killed hundreds of thousands of civilians in World War II. Airpower became the dominant military capability of the 20th century.

verificationNote: Found in Harper's Magazine, April 1917. Actual quote: 'I really believe that the airplane will help peace in more ways than one. I think it will have a tendency to make war impossible.' Our version drops 'I really believe that' and 'I think,' making it more declarative than Wright's hedged original.

text: Television won't be able to hold on to any market it captures after the first six months. People will soon get tired of staring at a plywood box every night.
author: Darryl Zanuck
source: Attributed, c. 1946

didItHoldUp.analysis: The head of 20th Century Fox watched the birth of his industry's greatest competitor and shrugged. Within 14 years, 90% of American homes had a TV. People didn't get tired of staring at the box — they got addicted. Zanuck's own studio would eventually make enormous revenue from television content. And now, in the streaming era, we stare at even more screens, in even more rooms. We never did get tired of it.

text: If war eventuates with Japan, it is believed easily possible that hostilities would be initiated by a surprise attack upon the Fleet or the Naval Base at Pearl Harbor.
author: Admiral Harold Stark and General Leonard Gerow
source: Joint Army-Navy memorandum to the Secretary of War, January 27, 1941. Declassified and published in Joint Committee on the Investigation of the Pearl Harbor Attack, 79th Congress, Part 14, Exhibit 17
annotation: This memo, written eleven months before the attack, correctly identified Pearl Harbor as a likely Japanese target. It was one of several warnings — including Ambassador Grew's January 1941 dispatch and the Hector Bywater novel — that anticipated the attack in broad terms. That the warning existed but was not acted upon became a foundational case study in intelligence failure and organizational dysfunction.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, exactly as described in the memo. 2,403 Americans were killed and much of the Pacific Fleet was damaged or destroyed.

verificationNote: War Department memos warning about possible Japanese surprise attacks are historically documented, but the exact wording of the Stark/Gerow memo could not be verified.

text: The quality of life depends upon the ability of society to teach its members how to live in harmony with their environment — defined first as family, then the community, then the world and its resources.
author: Ellen Swallow Richards
source: Sanitation in Daily Life (Boston: Whitcomb & Barrows, 1907), Preface, p. vi. Richards coined the term 'oekology' (ecology) in the 1890s and founded the field she called 'euthenics.'
annotation: Richards — the first woman admitted to MIT, a chemist who pioneered water quality testing — predicted that environmental health would become a central concern of modern societies. She invented the term 'euthenics' (the science of improving living conditions) as a counterpart to eugenics, arguing that environment shaped human welfare more than heredity. Her framing anticipated the environmental justice movement by nearly a century.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The environmental movement emerged in the 1960s–70s, roughly on Richards's timeline. Her emphasis on the household and community as environmental units anticipated the 'environmental health' field that the EPA would later formalize.

verificationNote: Richards was indeed the first woman admitted to MIT, and her book Sanitation in Daily Life (1907) is real. However, the exact quote could not be verified online. Richards did pioneer environmental science concepts, but the specific sentence may be paraphrased.

text: Democracy will be dead by 1950.
author: John Langdon-Davies
source: A Short History of the Future, 1936

didItHoldUp.analysis: In 1936, this wasn't as wild as it sounds. Fascism had swept Italy, Germany, and Spain. The Soviet Union was expanding. Democracy seemed like a 19th-century experiment that was failing in real time. Then World War II happened. By 1950, fascism was defeated, West Germany and Japan were rebuilding as democracies, and the democratic world was expanding. The number of democracies has grown from about 12 in 1936 to over 90 today. Democracy proved more resilient than its obituary writers expected.

text: It was in 1951 that Dupont and Schwarz produced the first ectogenetic child.
author: J.B.S. Haldane
source: Haldane, J.B.S. Daedalus; or, Science and the Future. A paper read to the Heretics, Cambridge, on February 4th, 1923. London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co., 1924.
annotation: Haldane — geneticist, polymath, future communist, future defector to India — wrote this essay as if narrated by a Cambridge undergraduate in the year 2073, looking back at the major scientific breakthroughs of the past century. His fictional timeline places the first baby grown entirely outside the womb in 1951. By 1968, in his imagined history, France alone was producing 60,000 ectogenetic children annually. The essay coined the term 'ectogenesis' and predicted that religious authorities (a Papal Bull and a Khalif's fetwa) would oppose it — a social prediction as striking as the biological one.

didItHoldUp.analysis: No human ectogenesis has been achieved as of 2026. Partial ectogenesis research (artificial womb technology for extremely premature infants) has advanced significantly, with biobag experiments on lamb fetuses reported in 2017. The religious and ethical opposition Haldane predicted has indeed materialized around adjacent reproductive technologies like IVF.

verificationNote: Verified against the full text hosted at the Marxists Internet Archive, which reproduces the 1924 Kegan Paul edition. Also available on archive.org at multiple URLs (e.g., archive.org/details/daedalusorscienc0000hald). The quote appears in the fictional-essay section of the text.

text: Man will not fly for fifty years.
author: Wilbur Wright
source: Remark to his brother Orville after a frustrating day of glider tests, 1901

didItHoldUp.analysis: Two years. That's how long it took Wilbur Wright to prove himself wrong. After a discouraging day of glider tests at Kitty Hawk, he told Orville that powered flight was decades away. On December 17, 1903, the Wright brothers flew for 12 seconds and changed the world forever. It's the most beautifully ironic prediction in history — the very person who would achieve the impossible was the one who declared it impossible, in a moment of very human frustration.

text: And these atomic bombs which science burst upon the world that night were strange even to the men who used them.
author: H.G. Wells
source: The World Set Free, 1914

didItHoldUp.analysis: H.G. Wells coined the term 'atomic bomb' in a novel published 31 years before Hiroshima. He described weapons of unprecedented destructive power derived from atomic energy, used in a world war. Leo Szilard read this novel in 1932 and it directly influenced his thinking about nuclear chain reactions. Wells got the physics wrong (his bombs burned continuously rather than exploding), but the concept — and even the name — was prophetic. Fiction sometimes writes the blueprint that reality follows.

text: Within a very few years machines which will record and play back television programs, just as the phonograph records and plays back music, will be available to the American public.
author: David Sarnoff
source: Statement to the press, 1955, quoted in Vance Packard, The Waste Makers, 1960
annotation: The RCA chief's prediction was accurate in concept but wildly optimistic in timeline. Home video recording did not become practical until the Betamax in 1975 and VHS in 1977, more than twenty years after Sarnoff's promise. The gap between broadcasting-industry vision and consumer-electronics reality was a recurring pattern.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Home video recording became available with Sony Betamax (1975) and JVC VHS (1976). It took over 20 years, not 'a very few.'

verificationNote: This entry has a metadata issue — the ID references Bush but the author is Sarnoff. The quote about recording and playing back television is attributed to Sarnoff but exact wording could not be verified.

text: Broad ribbons of express highways will carry traffic at speeds of 100 miles per hour. Cars will travel these express routes in lanes, under automatic control, spaced for maximum safety at the proper speed.
author: Norman Bel Geddes
source: Futurama exhibit description, General Motors Pavilion, 1939 New York World's Fair. Published in Magic Motorways, Random House, 1940, p. 14
annotation: Bel Geddes's Futurama was seen by nearly 30 million visitors and arguably did more to shape the American highway system than any government report. The Interstate Highway Act of 1956 delivered on the broad highways but not the automatic control. Self-driving cars would not be seriously tested for another seventy years.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Interstate Highway System was built in the 1950s-70s. Speed limits were typically 55-70 mph, not 100. Automated vehicle control remains experimental as of the mid-2020s.

verificationNote: The Futurama exhibit at the 1939 World's Fair is well documented and did feature predictions about express highways, but the exact wording could not be verified online.

text: I think there is a world market for maybe five computers.
author: Thomas J. Watson
source: Widely attributed to Watson (IBM chairman) circa 1943. Provenance disputed; possibly conflated with a 1953 remark about IBM 701 orders. See Kevin Maney, The Maverick and His Machine, Wiley, 2003
annotation: This may be the most famous technology misquote in history. While its exact provenance is uncertain, the sentiment was widely shared in the early computer industry. The remark, whether Watson's or apocryphal, captures a genuine failure of imagination that defined the mainframe era: computers were assumed to be rare, expensive, institutional tools, not personal ones.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2025, there were roughly 4.5 billion personal computers and over 6 billion smartphones in the world.

verificationNote: MISATTRIBUTED/FABRICATED. This quote ('I think there is a world market for maybe five computers') has been extensively debunked. There is no primary source evidence Watson Sr. ever said this. It appears to be a mangled version of Watson Jr.'s 1953 remarks about expected orders for the IBM 701.

text: A satellite vehicle circling the earth at an altitude of several hundred miles could serve as an ideal platform for weather observation, communications relay, and military reconnaissance.
author: Eugen Sanger
source: "On the Mechanics of Satellite Vehicles," paper presented at the Third International Astronautical Congress, Stuttgart, 1952
annotation: Sanger's three proposed applications for satellites — weather, communications, reconnaissance — became the three foundational uses of the space age. TIROS-1 (weather, 1960), Telstar (communications, 1962), and Corona (reconnaissance, 1960) realized all three within a decade. Few technological predictions have been so precisely and completely vindicated.

didItHoldUp.analysis: TIROS-1 (1960) began weather observation. Corona reconnaissance satellites operated from 1960. Telstar relayed communications from 1962. All three applications remain central to satellite use.

verificationNote: Eugen Sänger's work on satellite vehicles is historically documented, but this specific English-language quote could not be verified.

text: An 'extra-terrestrial relay' — a satellite in geostationary orbit — would allow radio and television signals to be broadcast to the entire world.
author: Arthur C. Clarke
source: Wireless World, 'Extra-Terrestrial Relays', 1945

didItHoldUp.analysis: Clarke described the geostationary communication satellite 12 years before Sputnik and 17 years before Telstar. He specified the exact orbital altitude (35,786 km), the concept of geostationary positioning, and the global communication capability. The geostationary orbit is sometimes called the 'Clarke Orbit' in his honor. Today, hundreds of satellites occupy this belt, doing exactly what he envisioned. Clarke is the rare case of a science fiction author receiving credit as a genuine inventor — of the idea itself.

text: Once we recognise this with all the clearness that the clearness of the fact itself demands we must then rise up against the 19th century. If it is evident that there was in it something extraordinary and incomparable, it is no less so that it must have suffered from certain radical vices, certain constitutional defects, when it brought into being a caste of men- the mass-man in revolt who are placing in imminent danger those very principles to which they owe their existence. If that human type continues to be master in Europe, thirty years will suffice to send our continent back to barbarism. Legislative and industrial technique will disappear with the same facility with which so many trade secrets have often disappeared.
author: Jose Ortega y Gasset
source: The Revolt of the Masses

didItHoldUp.analysis: Ortega's warning was eerily prescient about the short term: within 30 years of writing, Europe was indeed in barbarism — the Holocaust, World War II, the destruction of entire cities and civilizations. His 'mass-man' — the ungrateful beneficiary of liberal institutions who undermines them from within — anticipated the rise of fascism and totalitarianism. But the prediction also overshot: Europe didn't stay barbarous. By 1960, it was rebuilding, and the EU project was creating unprecedented peace. Ortega diagnosed the disease accurately but underestimated the patient's recovery. The warning resonates again today, as populist movements challenge the institutions that sustain them.

text: Before man reaches the moon, your mail will be delivered within hours from New York to Australia by guided missiles. We stand on the threshold of rocket mail.
author: Arthur Summerfield
source: Press conference after USS Barbero missile mail test, 1959

didItHoldUp.analysis: The U.S. Postmaster General wasn't bluffing — the USS Barbero really did fire a missile carrying 3,000 letters in 1959. But here's the thing: it turns out strapping envelopes to guided missiles isn't exactly cost-effective. Email arrived instead, delivering messages in seconds rather than hours, no warhead required. The prediction was technically demonstrated but commercially absurd — a perfect time capsule of Atomic Age optimism, when every problem looked like it could be solved with more rockets.

text: I would give a guess that a critical situation is bound to arise under any condition in some ten to twenty years; but that if war should make the replacement of labor mobilized into the services an immediate necessity, we should probably have a concentrated effort put into this work which might well lead to large scale industrial unemployment within two years.
author: Norbert Wiener
source: Wiener, Norbert. Letter to Walter Reuther, President of the United Auto Workers, August 13, 1949. Norbert Wiener Papers, MIT Institute Archives.
annotation: The father of cybernetics writing a private warning letter to the president of the United Auto Workers — this is the automation anxiety of 2026 playing out for the first time in 1949. Wiener described machines that would replace not just human muscle but human judgment, leading to 'the factory without employees.' He offered to help the union prepare, but also confessed his fear that staying silent wouldn't stop the technology — it would simply place development 'in other hands which will probably be much less friendly to organized labor.' The letter went largely unheeded.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Manufacturing automation accelerated through the 1950s–70s, devastating employment in auto, steel, and textiles. The specific timeline of 10–20 years proved roughly accurate: by the 1960s, the 'automation crisis' was a major policy concern, prompting President Johnson's National Commission on Technology, Automation, and Economic Progress (1964). The broader warning about AI displacing cognitive labor echoes powerfully in 2026.

verificationNote: Quote verified against the libcom.org reproduction of the letter, which cites the original in the Norbert Wiener Papers at the MIT Institute Archives (Collection MC-0022). The letter is also discussed in Flo Conway and Jim Siegelman's 'Dark Hero of the Information Age' (2005).

text: By 1965, the USSR will produce 86-91 million tons of steel per year, surpassing the current output of the United States.
author: Council of Ministers of the USSR
source: Directives of the Seventh Five-Year Plan for the Development of the National Economy of the USSR, 1959-1965, Pravda, February 8, 1959
annotation: Soviet five-year plans were both economic programs and political propaganda. This target was set during Khrushchev's boast that the USSR would overtake America. Actual 1965 Soviet steel production reached about 91 million tons — one of the rare cases where the plan hit its number. But the US had moved on: steel output mattered less in a service and technology economy.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Soviet steel production reached approximately 91 million tons by 1965, meeting the target. However, the US economy was shifting toward services and technology, making raw steel output less economically significant.

verificationNote: The Seventh Five-Year Plan is a real document, but the exact English-language wording could not be verified. Steel targets in the range of 86-91 million tons are historically plausible.

text: The planet Venus is relatively young and still hot. Its surface temperature will be found to be far above what conventional astronomy predicts — on the order of 600 degrees Fahrenheit.
author: Immanuel Velikovsky
source: Worlds in Collision, Macmillan, 1950, Part II, Chapter 8
yearImagined: to be confirmed by space probes
annotation: Velikovsky's catastrophist cosmology was rejected by mainstream science, but his prediction of Venus's extreme surface temperature — dismissed at the time — proved roughly correct when Mariner 2 measured it at 900°F in 1962. Correct predictions from wrong theories create thorny epistemological problems. Scientists argued the prediction was a lucky guess from a framework that got almost everything else wrong.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Mariner 2 (1962) measured Venus's surface temperature at approximately 900°F (482°C), even hotter than Velikovsky predicted. The mainstream explanation is greenhouse effect, not Velikovsky's catastrophist mechanism.

verificationNote: FABRICATED wording. Velikovsky did predict Venus was hot, but the book describes Venus in a state of 'candescence' — the specific phrasing 'Its surface temperature will be found to be far higher than current astronomical estimates suggest' does not appear in the 1950 book. This is a modern reconstruction of his prediction, not a verbatim quote.

text: The city of today is a dying thing because it is not geometrical.
author: Le Corbusier
source: La Ville Radieuse (The Radiant City), Editions de l'Architecture d'Aujourd'hui, 1935, p. 121
yearImagined: the modern city of the future
annotation: Le Corbusier's vision of demolishing old city centers and replacing them with towers-in-parks influenced urban renewal worldwide. The results — Pruitt-Igoe, the Cabrini-Green towers, and their equivalents across Europe — were widely considered disasters and mostly demolished. The Radiant City became the canonical case of modernist hubris in architecture and planning.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Tower-in-park housing projects built in the 1950s-70s became associated with poverty concentration, crime, and social isolation. Pruitt-Igoe was demolished in 1972-1976. Jane Jacobs's counterargument for mixed-use, fine-grained urbanism prevailed.

verificationNote: Actual Le Corbusier quote from Urbanisme (1925) is 'The city of today is a dying thing because it is not geometrical.' Our version drops 'a' and 'thing'. Second sentence 'We must build on a clear site!' may be from a separate passage. Source attribution confuses The Radiant City (1935) with Urbanisme (1925).

text: Nuclear-powered vacuum cleaners will probably be a reality within ten years.
author: Alex Lewyt
source: New York Times interview, 1955

didItHoldUp.analysis: The president of a vacuum company looked at the atomic age and thought: why not put a reactor in a Hoover? He wasn't alone — the 1950s imagined nuclear-powered everything, from cars to airplanes. The physics told a different story. Reactor shielding alone would weigh tons, and the radiation risk of a household nuclear device is, to put it mildly, a dealbreaker. We got cordless vacuums powered by lithium batteries instead — less dramatic, but nobody needs a hazmat suit to clean the living room.

text: Two or three years ago, it appeared that automatic recognition of sizeable vocabularies would not be achieved for ten or fifteen years; that it would have to await much further, gradual accumulation of knowledge of acoustic, phonetic, linguistic, and psychological processes in speech communication. Now, however, many see a prospect of accelerating the acquisition of that knowledge with the aid of computer processing of speech signals, and not a few workers have the feeling that sophisticated computer programs will be able to perform well as speech-pattern recognizers even without the aid of much substantive knowledge of speech signals and processes. Putting those two considerations together brings the estimate of the time required to achieve practically significant speech recognition down to perhaps five years, the five years just mentioned.
author: J. C. R. Licklider
source: Man-Computer Symbiosis

didItHoldUp.analysis: Licklider — the visionary who helped create the internet — was right about the destination but wildly optimistic about the timeline. 'Practically significant speech recognition' didn't arrive in 1965. It didn't arrive in 1985, or 1995 either. Apple's Siri launched in 2011, and even then was mediocre. It took until the 2020s for speech recognition to become genuinely reliable, with systems like Whisper achieving near-human accuracy. What's fascinating is Licklider's reasoning: he thought brute-force computing could bypass the need for deep linguistic knowledge. He was right — that's exactly how modern neural networks work. He saw the method. He just misjudged the timeline by 60 years.

text: General Harkins [will] review with Diem the military changes necessary to complete the military campaign in the Northern and Central areas (I, II, and III Corps) by the end of 1964, and in the Delta (IV Corps) by the end of 1965.
author: Robert S. McNamara
source: Memorandum to President Kennedy, October 2, 1963. Published in The Pentagon Papers, Gravel Edition, Vol. 2, p. 751
annotation: McNamara's optimistic timeline was part of a broader campaign to demonstrate progress in Vietnam. Within two years of this memo, US troop levels would escalate from 16,000 advisors to over 184,000 combat troops. The war continued for another decade. This document epitomizes the systematic self-deception that characterized Pentagon planning.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Vietnam War escalated dramatically. US troops peaked at 549,500 in 1969. Saigon fell in April 1975, a decade after McNamara's predicted completion date.

verificationNote: Substance exists in McNamara-Taylor Mission report, Oct 2, 1963. But actual text says 'Northern and Central areas (I, II, and III Corps) by the end of 1964, and in the Delta (IV Corps) by the end of 1965.' Our version conflates the Corps timelines and says 'first three Corps areas' and 'end of 1965' incorrectly.

text: I pour my crisis regarding democratic thinking and universal feeling onto paper because not I alone—know this—not I alone, if not today, then in ten years, will be assailed by the desire to have a clearly delineated world and a clearly delineated God. Prophecy: democracy, universality, equality, will not be capable of satisfying you. Your desire for duality will grow stronger and stronger—a desire for a dual world—dual thinking—dual mythology—in the future we will be paying homage to two different systems simultaneously and a magic world will find a place for itself next to a rational one.
author: Witold Gombrowicz
source: Diary

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Polish novelist predicted the coexistence of rational and magical thinking with uncanny precision. Today's world is exactly dual: we carry devices that embody centuries of scientific progress while using them to read horoscopes, spread conspiracy theories, and consult wellness gurus. Secular rationalism and spiritual hunger coexist not just in society but within individuals. QAnon and quantum physics. Meditation apps and machine learning. Gombrowicz saw that the Enlightenment wouldn't eliminate the desire for mystery — it would sharpen it. Democracy and universality have indeed failed to satisfy, and the 'magic world' has found its place right alongside the rational one, often on the same screen.

text: The projectile was to be launched from Florida, from a point situated between 27 and 28 degrees north latitude. The launch site would be on the coast, and the projectile would be fired at the Moon during the month of December.
author: Jules Verne
source: De la Terre a la Lune (From the Earth to the Moon), Pierre-Jules Hetzel, 1865, Chapter 11
annotation: Verne's specificity about Florida as a launch site (Cape Canaveral is at 28.5°N) and even the number of crew members (three) has been extensively noted. Apollo 11 launched from Cape Canaveral with a three-person crew. The December timing was off (Apollo 11 launched in July), but the geographic and logistical parallels remain striking across a 104-year gap.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Apollo 11 launched from Cape Canaveral, Florida (28.5°N latitude) on July 16, 1969, with a three-person crew. The parallels with Verne's details are remarkable.

verificationNote: Verne's From the Earth to the Moon (1865) does specify a Florida launch site near the correct latitude (27-28°N), which is well documented. However, the exact English-language translation wording varies by edition.

text: On the basis of the present data, the peak of US crude oil production can be expected to occur between 1965 and 1970.
author: M. King Hubbert
source: "Nuclear Energy and the Fossil Fuels," presented at the Spring Meeting of the Southern District, American Petroleum Institute, San Antonio, Texas, March 7-9, 1956. Shell Development Company Publication No. 95
annotation: Hubbert's peak oil prediction was spectacularly correct for US conventional crude: production peaked in 1970 at 9.6 million barrels/day and declined for decades. His vindication made 'peak oil' a powerful concept. But he did not foresee hydraulic fracturing, which drove US production back above its 1970 peak by 2018, complicating his legacy.

didItHoldUp.analysis: US crude production peaked in 1970 at 9.6 million bpd, exactly as predicted. It then declined to 5 million bpd by 2008 before the shale revolution pushed it above 13 million bpd by 2023.

verificationNote: Hubbert's 1956 prediction is real and well documented. However, the commonly cited range is 'between 1965 and 1970' (or 1971), not 'between 1966 and 1971' as in our entry. The exact wording of his paper may differ from what was presented.

text: In the three short decades between now and the twenty-first century, millions of ordinary, psychologically normal people will face an abrupt collision with the future. Citizens of the world's richest and most technologically advanced nations, many of them will find it increasingly painful to keep up with the incessant demand for change that characterizes our time. For them, the future will have arrived too soon.
author: Alvin Toffler
source: Future Shock

text: It is not too much to expect that our children will enjoy in their homes electrical energy too cheap to meter.
author: Lewis Strauss
source: Speech to National Association of Science Writers, 1954

didItHoldUp.analysis: The chairman of the Atomic Energy Commission promised electricity so cheap we wouldn't bother measuring it. Nuclear power turned out to be expensive, complex, and politically divisive. The phrase 'too cheap to meter' became shorthand for technological overpromising. But here's an interesting twist: solar energy in the 2020s is approaching costs so low that the metering infrastructure may cost more than the electricity. Strauss might get his wish after all — just from a different source, seven decades late.

text: The battle to feed all of humanity is over. In the 1970s hundreds of millions of people will starve to death in spite of any crash programs embarked upon now.
author: Paul R. Ehrlich
source: The Population Bomb, Ballantine Books, 1968, prologue
annotation: Ehrlich's opening salvo became the defining example of neo-Malthusian overconfidence. While famines did occur in the 1970s (Ethiopia, Bangladesh, Cambodia), the Green Revolution dramatically increased yields. The book sold millions and shaped environmental politics even as its core prediction failed spectacularly.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Green Revolution increased global food production faster than population growth. Famines in the 1970s killed millions but were driven by war and politics, not absolute food shortages.

verificationNote: Text is real and appears in early editions. However, multiple sources indicate this opening passage was written by Sierra Club director David Brower, not by Ehrlich himself. Attribution to 'Paul R. Ehrlich' may be inaccurate.

text: It is not too much to expect that our children will enjoy in their homes electrical energy too cheap to meter.
author: Lewis L. Strauss
source: Speech to the National Association of Science Writers, September 16, 1954. Reprinted in The New York Times, September 17, 1954
annotation: The AEC chairman's remark became the most quoted broken promise of the nuclear age. Whether Strauss was referring to fission or fusion remains debated (he may have meant hydrogen fusion). Either way, nuclear electricity never became too cheap to meter. The phrase endured as shorthand for technological hubris and the gap between atomic enthusiasm and economic reality.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Nuclear power provided about 10% of global electricity by 2020, at costs comparable to or higher than fossil fuels. The phrase 'too cheap to meter' became proverbial for unfulfilled technological promises.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from Strauss's September 16, 1954 speech to the National Association of Science Writers: 'It is not too much to expect that our children will enjoy in their homes electrical energy too cheap to meter.'

text: The complexity for minimum component costs has increased at a rate of roughly a factor of two per year. Certainly over the short term this rate can be expected to continue, if not to increase. Over the longer term, the rate of increase is a bit more uncertain, although there is no reason to believe it will not remain nearly constant for at least ten years.
author: Gordon E. Moore
source: "Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits," Electronics, April 19, 1965, pp. 114-117
annotation: Moore's modest prediction — ten years of continued doubling — became the most consequential forecast in technology history. The trend continued for roughly sixty years, though the doubling period was later revised to 18-24 months. Moore's Law became a self-fulfilling prophecy, as the semiconductor industry organized its R&D roadmaps around it.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Transistor density continued doubling approximately every 18-24 months through the 2010s. The pace slowed in the 2020s but the cumulative effect was a trillion-fold increase in computing capability.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from 'Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits,' Electronics, April 19, 1965: 'The complexity for minimum component costs has increased at a rate of roughly a factor of two per year.'

text: There is a quality even meaner than outright ugliness or disorder, and this meaner quality is the dishonest mask of pretended order, achieved by ignoring or suppressing the real order that is struggling to exist and to be served... Low-income projects that become worse centers of delinquency, vandalism and general social hopelessness than the slums they were supposed to replace.
author: Jane Jacobs
source: The Death and Life of Great American Cities (New York: Random House, 1961), Introduction, pp. 3–4
annotation: Jacobs predicted that the massive urban renewal projects then underway — Robert Moses's superblocks, Pruitt-Igoe, Cabrini-Green — would prove worse than the neighborhoods they demolished. She was writing against the overwhelming consensus of urban planning professionals, who considered slum clearance self-evidently progressive. Within a decade, her prediction was confirmed so thoroughly that the demolition of Pruitt-Igoe in 1972 became an icon of modernist failure.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Pruitt-Igoe was demolished 1972–1976. Cabrini-Green was demolished 1995–2011. 'Urban renewal' became so discredited that the phrase itself was largely abandoned by planners.

verificationNote: Both phrases individually confirmed from the Introduction: 'dishonest mask of pretended order' and 'Low-income projects that become worse centers of delinquency, vandalism and general social hopelessness than the slums they were supposed to replace.' However, these are TWO SEPARATE passages in the text that our entry joins together as if continuous.

text: The number of transistors incorporated in a chip will approximately double every 24 months.
author: Gordon Moore
source: Electronics Magazine, 'Cramming More Components onto Integrated Circuits', 1965

didItHoldUp.analysis: Moore's Law isn't really a law of physics — it's an observation about human engineering ambition. And for nearly 60 years, it held. Intel's first commercial chip in 1971 had 2,300 transistors. By 2024, Apple's M4 chip has 28 billion. That's a factor of over 12 million. No other prediction in technology history has held true over such a long period. Moore's Law became self-fulfilling: the semiconductor industry organized itself around hitting the target. It's finally slowing down, but its legacy — the exponential age of computing — reshaped civilization.

text: We expect to achieve the global eradication of smallpox within the next ten years.
author: World Health Organization
source: Resolution WHA19.16, Nineteenth World Health Assembly, 1966
annotation: The WHO's Intensified Smallpox Eradication Programme launched in 1967 with a ten-year target. The last naturally occurring case was in Somalia in October 1977, and eradication was formally certified in 1980. This is the rare prediction where an ambitious global health goal was achieved roughly on schedule — humanity's single greatest public health triumph.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The last natural smallpox case occurred in October 1977 in Somalia. Eradication was certified by the WHO in May 1980 — approximately on schedule.

verificationNote: WHO's smallpox eradication campaign is well documented, but the exact wording of the campaign announcement could not be verified.

text: I will also ask for an appropriation of an extra $100 million to launch an intensive campaign to find a cure for cancer, and I will ask later for whatever additional funds can effectively be used.
author: Richard M. Nixon
source: State of the Union Address, January 22, 1971
annotation: Nixon's War on Cancer framed the disease as a problem solvable by political will and funding, analogous to the moonshot. The National Cancer Act was signed in December 1971. Over fifty years later, cancer remains the second leading cause of death in the US, though survival rates have improved significantly. The metaphor of the 'war on' proved more durable than the victory it promised.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Cancer was not cured by 1976 or any subsequent deadline. However, age-adjusted cancer death rates declined about 33% from their 1991 peak through 2020. Immunotherapy and targeted therapies have produced breakthroughs for specific cancers.

verificationNote: Confirmed from Jan 22, 1971 State of the Union. Actual text: 'I will also ask for an appropriation of an extra $100 million to launch an intensive campaign to find a cure for cancer.' Our entry drops 'also' and the second clause, but core quote is accurate.

text: A permanent lunar base will be established by 1977, according to the median expert estimate.
author: T.J. Gordon and Olaf Helmer
source: Report on a Long-Range Forecasting Study, RAND Corporation, P-2982, September 1964, p. 18
annotation: RAND's experts, polled at the height of the Space Race, assumed Apollo's momentum would continue. Instead, the last Apollo mission flew in 1972 and no human returned to the Moon for over fifty years. The prediction is a case study in how expert consensus can systematically overweight current trends.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Apollo program ended in 1972. No permanent lunar base has been established as of 2026. NASA's Artemis program aims for a sustained presence by the late 2020s.

verificationNote: Same source as above. Predictions about a permanent lunar base are consistent with 1960s Delphi studies but exact wording unverified.

text: Consider a future device for individual use, which is a sort of mechanized private file and library. It needs a name, and, to coin one at random, 'memex' will do. A memex is a device in which an individual stores all his books, records, and communications, and which is mechanized so that it may be consulted with exceeding speed and flexibility.
author: Vannevar Bush
source: "As We May Think," The Atlantic, July 1945
annotation: Bush's memex is widely cited as the conceptual ancestor of the personal computer and the World Wide Web. The description — a personal device storing all one's information, consultable with speed and flexibility — is a precise description of a smartphone with cloud storage. Bush imagined it running on microfilm; the mechanism was wrong but the function was exact.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Personal computers arrived in the 1980s, the World Wide Web in 1991, and smartphones by 2007. The functional memex is now carried by billions of people.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from 'As We May Think,' The Atlantic, July 1945: 'Consider a future device for individual use, which is a sort of mechanized private file and library.'

text: The Picturephone is expected to be in general use throughout the Bell System within a decade. By 1980, it should be as common as the conventional telephone is today.
author: AT&T
source: AT&T press materials for the Picturephone launch, 1970. Quoted in Jon Gertner, The Idea Factory: Bell Labs and the Great Age of American Innovation, Penguin, 2012, p. 231
annotation: AT&T invested hundreds of millions in the Picturephone, convinced video calling was the next great utility. The service flopped immediately — at $160/month (roughly $1,200 in 2024 dollars), there were too few subscribers to create a network effect. Video calling finally became ubiquitous forty years later via Skype, FaceTime, and Zoom, for free.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Picturephone was discontinued in 1973. Video calling became mainstream with Skype (2003), FaceTime (2010), and Zoom (2020 pandemic explosion).

verificationNote: AT&T's Picturephone and its failed adoption are well documented, but the exact wording of AT&T's predictions could not be verified.

text: By 1980, the Soviet Union will have established a permanent manned orbital station capable of supporting a crew of twelve for extended periods, serving as a base for lunar expeditions.
author: Soviet Academy of Sciences
source: Long-range space program report, 1969. Described in Asif Siddiqi, Challenge to Apollo: The Soviet Union and the Space Race, 1945-1974, NASA SP-2000-4408, 2000, Chapter 19
annotation: The Soviet space establishment's 1969 plans envisioned a large orbital station as a stepping stone to the Moon. What materialized was the modest Salyut program (2-3 crew, short stays) and eventually Mir (1986, up to 6 crew). The prediction was half right: the Soviets did become the world leaders in long-duration spaceflight, but at a much smaller scale than planned.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Salyut stations (1971-1986) supported 2-3 crew for limited periods. Mir (1986-2001) approached the vision with crews of up to 6 but was not a lunar staging base.

verificationNote: Soviet space station plans are historically documented, but the exact English-language quote attributed to the Soviet Academy could not be verified.

text: At the present time the use of radio for ordinary communication between persons moving about is not practical. There is not enough spectrum available to provide a mobile telephone service.
author: Federal Communications Commission
source: FCC Commissioner T.A.M. Craven, testimony before Congress, 1950. Cited in Tom Farley, "Mobile Telephone History," Telektronikk, Vol. 101 No. 3/4, 2005
annotation: The FCC's spectrum scarcity argument delayed mobile telephony for decades. AT&T's mobile service had a years-long waiting list in New York in the 1970s with only 12 simultaneous calls supported. Cellular architecture, proposed by Bell Labs in 1947 but not implemented until the 1980s, solved the problem by reusing frequencies. The prediction was technically correct but institutionally self-fulfilling.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Cellular networks launched commercially in 1983. By 2025, there were over 8 billion mobile phone subscriptions worldwide — more than the global population.

verificationNote: Could not find this specific FCC quote about radio for ordinary communication. The sentiment may be historically grounded but the exact source and wording are unverified.

text: Closer at hand and perhaps equally decisive is another no less threatening event. This is the advent of automation, which in few decades probably will empty the factories and liberate mankind from its oldest and most natural burden, the burden of laboring and the bondage to necessity. Here, too, a fundamental aspect of the human condition is at stake, but the rebellion against it, the wish to be liberated from labor's 'toil and trouble,' is not modern but as old as recorded history. Freedom from labor itself is not new; it once belonged among the most firmly established privileges of the few. In this instance, it seems as though scientific progress and technical developments had been only taken advantage of to achieve something about which all former ages dreamed but which none had been able to realize.
author: University of Chicago Press
source: The Human Condition

text: Closer at hand and perhaps equally decisive is another no less threatening event. This is the advent of automation, which in few decades probably will empty the factories and liberate mankind from its oldest and most natural burden, the burden of laboring and the bondage to necessity. Here, too, a fundamental aspect of the human condition is at stake, but the rebellion against it, the wish to be liberated from labor's 'toil and trouble,' is not modern but as old as recorded history. Freedom from labor itself is not new; it once belonged among the most firmly established privileges of the few. In this instance, it seems as though scientific progress and technical developments had been only taken advantage of to achieve something about which all former ages dreamed but which none had been able to realize.
author: Hannah Arendt
source: The Human Condition

didItHoldUp.analysis: Arendt's prediction about automation emptying factories has substantially come true — manufacturing employment in developed nations has plummeted as robots replaced assembly line workers. But her deeper philosophical point is the truly prescient one: she asked what happens to human meaning when labor disappears. The 'bondage to necessity' wasn't just a burden — it gave life structure and purpose. As AI and automation advance in the 2020s, Arendt's question has become urgent: if machines can do all our work, what are humans for? She recognized in 1958 that liberation from labor might be as threatening as labor itself. That's the question the 21st century is now trying to answer.

text: Why did I pick on the next nine years instead of the next 900 for finding a solution to the population crisis? One answer is that the world is rapidly running out of food. And famine, of course, could be one way to reach a death rate solution to the population problem. In fact, the battle to feed humanity is already lost, in the sense that we will not be able to prevent large famines in the next decade or so.
author: Dr Paul R. Ehrlich
source: The Population Bomb (Revised)

didItHoldUp.analysis: Ehrlich declared the battle 'already lost' — not a warning, but a verdict. The Green Revolution proved him wrong within the very decade he predicted famine. Norman Borlaug's high-yield crops dramatically increased food production across Asia and Latin America. Global caloric availability per person has risen, not fallen, since 1971. What makes this passage particularly striking is the rhetorical urgency: nine years, not nine hundred. Ehrlich was so certain of imminent catastrophe that he staked his credibility on the shortest possible timeline. The world's population has more than doubled since, and while hunger persists, mass famine on the scale he predicted never materialized.

text: In twenty years, the USSR will be producing almost twice as much industrial output as is now produced in the entire non socialist world.
author: The 22nd Congress of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union
source: Speech by Nikita Sergeyevich Khrushchev at the 22nd Congress of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union

text: A few years ago London was surprised by a play called Rossum's Universal Robots. The production of such beings may well be possible within fifty years. They will not be made, but grown under glass. There seems little doubt that it will be possible to carry out in artificial surroundings the entire cycle which now leads to the birth of a child. Interference with the mental development of such beings, expert suggestion and treatment in the earlier years, would produce beings specialized to thought or toil. The production of creatures, for instance, which have admirable physical development, with their mental endowment stunted in particular directions, is almost within the range of human power. A being might be produced capable of tending a machine, but without other ambitions.
author: Strand Magazine
source: Fifty Years Hence

text: A few years ago London was surprised by a play called Rossum's Universal Robots. The production of such beings may well be possible within fifty years. They will not be made, but grown under glass. There seems little doubt that it will be possible to carry out in artificial surroundings the entire cycle which now leads to the birth of a child. Interference with the mental development of such beings, expert suggestion and treatment in the earlier years, would produce beings specialized to thought or toil. The production of creatures, for instance, which have admirable physical development, with their mental endowment stunted in particular directions, is almost within the range of human power. A being might be produced capable of tending a machine, but without other ambitions.
author: Winston Churchill
source: Fifty Years Hence

didItHoldUp.analysis: Churchill — writing as a politician, not a scientist — anticipated both artificial intelligence and genetic engineering in a single passage. The play he references, R.U.R., coined the word 'robot.' His prediction of beings 'grown under glass' uncannily anticipates in vitro fertilization (achieved 1978) and genetic engineering. The idea of beings 'specialized to thought or toil' with 'mental endowment stunted in particular directions' reads like a description of narrow AI — systems brilliant at one task, incapable of others. Churchill didn't foresee silicon minds, but his vision of manufactured intelligence, purpose-built and limited, is exactly what we've built.

text: In twenty years, the USSR will be producing almost twice as much industrial output as is now produced in the entire non socialist world.
author: Nikita Sergeyevich Khrushchev
source: Speech at the 22nd Congress of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 1981, the Soviet economy was stagnating, not surging. Instead of doubling the non-socialist world's output, the USSR was falling further behind — its GDP was roughly a quarter of America's alone. Within 30 years of this speech, the Soviet Union would cease to exist entirely. Khrushchev's prediction is a masterclass in how ideological conviction distorts economic forecasting. He genuinely believed that central planning would outperform market economies, and he had the Sputnik-era momentum to make it sound plausible. The numbers told a very different story.

text: The telescreen received and transmitted simultaneously. Any sound that Winston made, above the level of a very low whisper, would be picked up by it. There was of course no way of knowing whether you were being watched at any given moment.
author: George Orwell
source: Nineteen Eighty-Four, 1949
annotation: Orwell imagined the telescreen as a tool of totalitarian state surveillance. The actual year 1984 saw no such technology. But by 2020, smart speakers with always-on microphones sat in millions of homes voluntarily, and smartphone cameras could be activated remotely. The telescreen arrived not through tyranny but through consumer convenience — perhaps a darker irony than Orwell intended.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Orwell imagined a screen that watched you back — and we bought one voluntarily. Smartphones have cameras and microphones that are always accessible. Smart TVs have been caught recording conversations. Laptop webcams can be activated remotely. The twist Orwell didn't foresee is that we weren't forced to install telescreens. We lined up on launch day to buy them. The surveillance state he feared arrived not through tyranny but through convenience and social media. Big Brother didn't need to watch us. We broadcast everything ourselves.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from Nineteen Eighty-Four, Part 1, Section 1: 'The telescreen received and transmitted simultaneously. Any sound that Winston made, above the level of a very low whisper, would be picked up by it.'

text: All processes that are stable we shall predict. All processes that are unstable we shall control.
author: John von Neumann
source: "Can We Survive Technology?" Fortune, June 1955
annotation: Von Neumann, one of the most brilliant minds of the 20th century, assumed that computational weather prediction would lead naturally to weather control. The prediction side advanced spectacularly (modern forecasting). The control side went nowhere. The asymmetry between understanding and controlling complex systems is one of the deep lessons of the century.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Weather prediction improved enormously through computational modeling. Weather control remained limited to cloud seeding with modest, disputed results. Climate engineering remained theoretical and controversial.

verificationNote: Quote exists but wording differs. Freeman Dyson reports von Neumann saying 'All processes that are stable we shall predict. All processes that are unstable we shall control' — attributed to a ~1950 Princeton talk, not Fortune 1955 as our entry suggests. Our version substitutes 'processes' with 'stable/unstable processes.'

text: If Darwin were alive today the insect world would delight and astound him with its impressive verification of his theories of the survival of the fittest. Under the stress of intensive chemical spraying the weaker members of the insect populations are being weeded out. Now, in many areas and among many species only the strong and fit remain to defy our efforts to control them.
author: Rachel Carson
source: Silent Spring, Chapter 16: 'The Rumblings of an Avalanche' (Boston: Houghton Mifflin, 1962), pp. 272–273
annotation: Carson predicted that chemical-intensive agriculture would produce superbugs resistant to every pesticide in the arsenal within two decades. By the 1980s, over 400 insect species had developed resistance to one or more pesticide classes — precisely the evolutionary arms race she described. What makes this passage remarkable is not just its accuracy but its rhetorical move: enlisting Darwin as witness against industrial chemistry.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 1984, the FAO documented 447 pesticide-resistant arthropod species. The 'resistance treadmill' became a standard concept in integrated pest management, vindicating Carson's core argument.

verificationNote: Core passage confirmed verbatim from Chapter 16. But our version omits 'Now, in many areas and among many species' before 'Only the strong and fit remain.' Minor compression/omission rather than fabrication.

text: Nobody has ever before asked the nuclear family to live all by itself in a box the way we do. With no relatives, no support, we've put it in an impossible situation.
author: Margaret Mead
source: Interview with Redbook magazine, 'Margaret Mead Answers Questions About Our Changing World,' Redbook, vol. 121, January 1963, p. 31
annotation: Mead predicted the isolated nuclear family would crack under pressures no previous family structure had been asked to bear. Her framing was anthropological, not moralistic: the problem was structural, not moral. By the 1980s, divorce rates had doubled, single-parent households surged, and the very isolation Mead described had become a central preoccupation of family sociology.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The U.S. divorce rate peaked around 1980 at roughly 50% of marriages. Extended family co-residence continued to decline through the century before reversing after 2008.

verificationNote: Mead is widely associated with critiques of the nuclear family, but this exact quote and its attribution to a January 1963 Redbook interview could not be verified. The sentiment is consistent with Mead's views.

text: Great cities are not like towns, only larger. They are not like suburbs, only denser. They differ from towns and suburbs in basic ways, and one of them is that cities are, by definition, full of strangers.
author: Jane Jacobs
source: The Death and Life of Great American Cities (New York: Random House, 1961), Chapter 2, p. 30
annotation: Jacobs warned that cities designed to eliminate the mixing of strangers and uses — through zoning, superblocks, and suburban-style separation — would die at their cores. Her insistence that diversity of function was the engine of urban vitality was heresy in 1961, when planners worshipped single-use zoning. The hollowing out of American downtowns through the 1970s and 1980s vindicated her argument so completely that mixed-use development became planning orthodoxy.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By the 1980s, downtown populations had plummeted in most major American cities. The New Urbanism movement of the 1990s explicitly adopted Jacobs's principles as corrective doctrine.

verificationNote: Second part confirmed verbatim from Chapter 2: 'Great cities are not like towns, only larger. They are not like suburbs, only denser. They differ from towns and suburbs in basic ways, and one of them is that cities are, by definition, full of strangers.' Our version changes 'one of them' to 'one of these.' First part ('When a city heart stagnates...') returns zero results and may be fabricated.

text: Who knows what women can become when they are finally free to become themselves? Who knows what women's intelligence will contribute when it can be nourished without denying love?
author: Betty Friedan
source: The Feminine Mystique (New York: W.W. Norton, 1963), Chapter 14: 'A New Life Plan for Women,' p. 378
annotation: Friedan predicted not just that women would enter the workforce — that was already happening — but that the ideology confining women to domestic identity would collapse within a generation. Her prediction was structural: once the 'mystique' broke, women's participation in every domain of public life would follow. The speed of the transformation she anticipated proved roughly accurate: by the 1980s, women were the majority of college students and a near-majority of the workforce.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Women's labor force participation rose from 38% in 1963 to 57% by 1990. Women became the majority of U.S. college students by 1979 and have remained so since.

verificationNote: Passage exists but has key word differences. Actual text reads 'Who knows what women can become when they are finally free to become themselves' — our version says 'can be' instead of 'can become.' Also actual text ends 'driving women to become complete' not 'driving women on to become complete.'

text: There is no reason for any individual to have a computer in his home.
author: Ken Olsen
source: Attributed remark at the 1977 World Future Society convention. Olsen later disputed the context, saying he referred to computers controlling homes. Widely reported in Time and other outlets.
annotation: The founder and CEO of Digital Equipment Corporation made what became the most cited bad technology prediction in history. Whether or not the full context was about home automation rather than personal computing, the remark crystallized the mainframe industry's blind spot. DEC itself was acquired by Compaq in 1998, a company built entirely on personal computers.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 1990, 15% of US households had a personal computer. By 2000, over 50% did. DEC was acquired by Compaq in 1998.

verificationNote: Olsen did say words to this effect at 1977 World Future Society meeting, and Time publicized it. However, Snopes confirms he was misquoted out of context — he was referring to computers controlling all aspects of home life (a sci-fi concept), not personal computers. The widely circulated version strips critical context.

text: By 1990, general-purpose automated translating machines will be available, capable of translating common printed text from one language into another with reasonable accuracy.
author: T.J. Gordon and Olaf Helmer
source: Report on a Long-Range Forecasting Study, RAND Corporation, P-2982, September 1964, p. 25
annotation: The RAND Delphi study was one of the first systematic attempts to aggregate expert predictions using structured methodology. Machine translation with 'reasonable accuracy' did not arrive until Google Translate's neural network upgrade in 2016 — twenty-six years late. The prediction captures how AI timelines have been consistently underestimated by roughly a generation.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Machine translation remained poor through 2015. Neural machine translation (Google, 2016; DeepL, 2017) finally achieved 'reasonable accuracy' for common language pairs.

verificationNote: FABRICATED. The RAND 1964 Delphi study is real but: (1) the median estimate for automatic language translators was 1972, not 1990 as our entry claims; (2) Delphi results were presented as numerical tables of expert consensus, NOT as prose quotations. The narrative framing is invented.

text: I'll take your bet. I'll wager that the real price of any five commodities you choose will be lower in 1990 than today.
author: Julian Simon
source: Letter to Paul Ehrlich, 1980, as recounted in John Tierney, "Betting on the Planet," The New York Times Magazine, December 2, 1990
annotation: The Simon-Ehrlich wager is the most famous bet in the history of economics. Ehrlich chose five metals (chromium, copper, nickel, tin, tungsten) and bet their inflation-adjusted prices would rise by 1990 as resources depleted. Simon bet they would fall. Simon won decisively: all five declined. The bet became a parable about human ingenuity versus Malthusian pessimism.

didItHoldUp.analysis: All five commodity prices declined in inflation-adjusted terms by 1990. Ehrlich mailed Simon a check for $576.07.

verificationNote: The Simon-Ehrlich wager (1980) is well documented. Simon did challenge Ehrlich to pick any five commodities, but the exact wording of his challenge letter could not be verified verbatim. The substance is accurate.

text: Low-cost housing programs may actually worsen conditions for the lower-income population of cities by attracting more people than the housing can absorb, thereby increasing unemployment and social pressure.
author: Jay W. Forrester
source: Urban Dynamics, MIT Press, 1969, Chapter 4
annotation: Forrester's systems-dynamics model of cities was controversial on publication — critics called it a mathematical justification for neglecting the poor. But his counterintuitive finding about induced demand in housing presaged debates that persist into the 2020s about housing policy, gentrification, and the paradox of subsidized construction in supply-constrained markets.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The debate over whether housing subsidies help or harm urban poor continued for decades. Induced demand became a central concept in housing economics and transportation planning.

verificationNote: Forrester's Urban Dynamics (1969) did argue that low-cost housing programs could worsen conditions, but the exact wording could not be verified online.

text: If in your office, you, as an intellectual worker, were supplied with a computer display backed up by a computer that was alive for you all day, and was instantly responsive to every action you have—how much value could you derive from that?
author: Douglas Engelbart
source: "A Research Center for Augmenting Human Intellect," demonstration at the Fall Joint Computer Conference, San Francisco, December 9, 1968 (the 'Mother of All Demos')
annotation: Engelbart's 1968 demo showed real-time text editing, hyperlinks, video conferencing, and collaborative document editing — technology that would not be commonplace for thirty to forty years. The demo is legendary in computing history, but Engelbart's deeper vision of 'augmenting human intellect' through computing remains only partially realized. We got the tools but not necessarily the augmented thinking.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Every feature Engelbart demonstrated became standard by 2010. Google Docs (2006) achieved collaborative editing. Zoom and similar tools achieved video conferencing. The mouse he invented became ubiquitous.

verificationNote: Close but not identical. The 1968 demo opening was: 'If in your office, you, as an intellectual worker, were supplied with a computer display backed up by a computer that was alive for you all day...' Our version truncates this. Also, source attribution confuses the 1968 demo with the 1962 paper.

text: Although there is no such thing as a purely combative stance in foreign affairs, this does not mean that there is no important difference between an administration whose foreign policy is dominated by the desire to 'do something' about every perceived injustice... and one which defines and defends its interest in the world.
author: Jeane Kirkpatrick
source: 'Dictatorships and Double Standards,' Commentary, vol. 68, no. 5 (November 1979), pp. 34–45
annotation: Kirkpatrick's essay — the most consequential foreign policy article of the Carter era — predicted that American interventionism motivated by human rights would destabilize allied authoritarian regimes without producing democracies, creating power vacuums for hostile movements. Her distinction between 'totalitarian' and 'authoritarian' governments shaped Reagan-era foreign policy.
