annotation: Asimov's 2014 predictions, written for the Times on the occasion of the 1964 World's Fair, are a mixed bag of remarkable hits and misses. LED lighting panels became common consumer products by 2014, and Philips Hue (2012) allowed exactly the color-changing he described. This entry shows Asimov at his most precisely prescient.

didItHoldUp.analysis: LED panels and smart lighting (Philips Hue, 2012) made color-changing room lighting a commercial reality by 2014.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from 'Visit to the World's Fair of 2014,' New York Times, August 16, 1964: 'By 2014, electroluminescent panels will be in common use. Ceilings and walls will glow softly, and in a variety of colors that will change at the touch of a push button.'

text: Indeed, the most somber speculation I can make about A.D. 2014 is that in a society of enforced leisure, the most glorious single word in the vocabulary will have become work!
author: Isaac Asimov
source: "Visit to the World's Fair of 2014," The New York Times, August 16, 1964
annotation: Like Keynes before him, Asimov assumed automation would produce mass leisure. The actual 2014 saw the gig economy, the hustle-culture ethos, and stagnant wages forcing many into multiple jobs. Work had not become scarce; it had become precarious. The prediction reveals how mid-century thinkers universally misjudged capitalism's appetite for labor.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2014, the gig economy was expanding, work hours remained high, and 'hustle culture' was ascendant. Automation displaced specific jobs but did not reduce overall labor demand.

verificationNote: Confirmed in original NYT essay. Exact text: 'Indeed, the most somber speculation I can make about A.D. 2014 is that in a society of enforced leisure, the most glorious single word in the vocabulary will have become work!' Our entry truncates before the key conclusion about 'work.'

text: Gadgetry will continue to relieve mankind of tedious jobs. Kitchen units will be designed to prepare 'automeals,' heating water and converting it to coffee; toasting bread; frying, poaching or scrambling eggs, grilling bacon, and so on.
author: Isaac Asimov
source: Visit to the World's Fair of 2014, New York Times, 1964

didItHoldUp.analysis: Asimov nailed it. Keurig machines convert water to coffee at the push of a button. Smart toasters, instant pots, and air fryers handle most of these exact tasks. While we don't have a single 'automeal' unit, our kitchens are full of specialized gadgets that do precisely what he described. Even meal-kit delivery services and pre-prepped ingredients fit his vision of automated food preparation. Asimov wrote this for the 1964 World's Fair, imagining 2014 — and he was remarkably, delightfully right.

text: The world of A.D. 2014 will have few routine jobs that cannot be done better by some machine than by any human being. Mankind will therefore have become largely a race of machine tenders.
author: Isaac Asimov
source: Visit to the World's Fair of 2014, New York Times, 1964

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2014, automation had eliminated millions of routine manufacturing and clerical jobs. Self-checkout, robotic assembly lines, and algorithmic trading were all reality. But 'machine tenders' undersells what happened — many humans shifted to service, creative, and knowledge work rather than simply minding machines. The 2020s brought AI that threatens even those refuges. Asimov was early on the timeline but increasingly right about the trajectory. The question he raised — what do humans do when machines do everything? — is now the defining economic question of our era.

text: Communications will become sight-sound and you will see as well as hear the person you telephone. The screen can be used not only to see the people you call but also for studying documents and photographs and reading passages from books.
author: Isaac Asimov
source: Visit to the World's Fair of 2014, New York Times, 1964

didItHoldUp.analysis: Asimov described FaceTime, Zoom, and screen-sharing in 1964 — down to using the screen to review documents during a call. By 2014, video calling was commonplace. By 2020, it was essential. The pandemic made Asimov's vision mandatory for billions of workers, students, and grandparents. He even anticipated the multifunction screen: phone, document viewer, and book reader in one device. That's an iPhone. Written 43 years before Steve Jobs held one up on stage.

text: By about 2015, the current encryption methods based on the factoring of large prime numbers will likely become vulnerable due to advances in computing power and mathematical research.
author: National Security Agency (declassified assessment)
source: "The Future of Information Security," NSA internal assessment, 1999, partially declassified 2012 under FOIA
annotation: The NSA's internal forecast about cryptographic vulnerability proved premature — RSA encryption remained practically secure well past 2015. However, the concern was directionally correct: by the 2020s, the prospect of quantum computing breaking current encryption drove a global push toward post-quantum cryptography standards.

didItHoldUp.analysis: RSA-2048 remained secure through 2025. NIST began standardizing post-quantum cryptographic algorithms in 2022-2024, driven by anticipated (not yet realized) quantum threats.

verificationNote: Could not find any declassified NSA document with this exact text. The general topic of encryption vulnerability timelines is real, but this specific quote and source attribution could not be verified. Possibly fabricated.

text: After sixty years of devoted service, Moore's Law will die a dignified death no later than the year 2019. By that time, transistor features will be just a few atoms in width, and the strategy of ever finer photolithography will have run its course.
author: Ray Kurzweil
source: The Law of Accelerating Returns

didItHoldUp.analysis: Kurzweil was remarkably close. By 2019, traditional Moore's Law scaling had indeed slowed dramatically — transistor features were approaching atomic limits, and the classic strategy of shrinking everything was hitting physics walls. Intel struggled to move beyond 14nm for years. But Moore's Law didn't die so much as evolve: chipmakers found new tricks (3D stacking, chiplet architectures, new materials) that kept performance improving even as traditional scaling stalled. Kurzweil was right about the mechanism dying but underestimated the industry's ability to find workarounds. The dignified death he predicted turned out to be more of a dignified metamorphosis.

text: By 2019, we will be living in a paperless society.
author: Roger Smith
source: Various industry speeches, GM Chairman, 1986

didItHoldUp.analysis: Here's the punchline: paper use actually increased after computers arrived, because suddenly everyone could print everything. It peaked around 2007 and has been declining since. Digital signatures, e-tickets, online billing, and e-books have replaced a lot of paper. We're not paperless — but paper's role is dramatically diminished. COVID-19 accelerated the shift further. The prediction was directionally right but too absolute. We didn't go paperless. We went paper-light.

text: By 2020, $1,000 of computing will equal the processing power of the human brain.
author: Ray Kurzweil
source: The Singularity Is Near: When Humans Transcend Biology, Viking, 2005, p. 127
annotation: Kurzweil's prediction depended on two assumptions: exponential growth in computing hardware and a specific estimate of the brain's computational capacity (~10^16 operations per second). The hardware claim was roughly on track, but comparing FLOPS to neural computation remains deeply contested. The prediction reveals the gap between raw computation and functional intelligence.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2020, high-end GPUs approached 10^15 FLOPS for ~$1,000. Whether this equals 'brain processing power' depends entirely on disputed estimates of the brain's computational requirements.

verificationNote: Kurzweil made predictions about computing power matching the human brain in The Singularity Is Near (2005), but the exact dollar amount and year may be paraphrased. He used various formulations across different works.

text: Information, even when it is correct, can be too slow to arrive, can arrive at the wrong place, can arrive in unintelligible form, can be ignored, or can be denied. Any of those failures can allow a system to overshoot its limits.
author: Donella H. Meadows
source: Beyond the Limits: Confronting Global Collapse, Envisioning a Sustainable Future (White River Junction, VT: Chelsea Green, 1992), p. 178
annotation: In this sequel to Limits to Growth, Meadows dismantled the optimistic assumption that better information technology would automatically solve environmental crises. She predicted that the information age would not prevent overshoot because the problem was never informational — it was structural, political, and psychological. Written before the internet was public, this passage anticipated the paradox of the 2020s: unprecedented access to climate data coexisting with political paralysis.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Despite satellite monitoring, real-time emissions tracking, and near-universal scientific consensus, global CO₂ emissions continued to rise through the 2020s. The information was available; the system response lagged.

verificationNote: Beyond the Limits (1992) is a real sequel to Limits to Growth, and Meadows did discuss information delays as causes of overshoot. However, the exact wording could not be verified online.

text: I foresee a universal information system (UIS), which will give everyone access at any given moment to the contents of any book that has ever been published or any magazine or any fact.
author: Andrei Sakharov
source: Sakharov, Andrei. "Tomorrow: The View From Red Square." Saturday Review, Vol. 1, No. 25, August 24, 1974. Translated by Antonina W. Bouis.
annotation: A Soviet nuclear physicist and dissident, under surveillance by the KGB, telephoned this essay to an American magazine describing what is unmistakably the internet and Wikipedia — decades before either existed. The prediction appeared in the same article where Sakharov also correctly forecast world population exceeding 7 billion by 2024. That a man denied basic freedoms by his own government could envision universal access to all human knowledge is one of the great ironies of Cold War futurism.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The World Wide Web (1991) and subsequent search engines, digital libraries, and Wikipedia collectively realize Sakharov's UIS almost exactly as described. Global internet users exceeded 5 billion by 2024.

verificationNote: Verified against the Sakharov Foundation's digital reproduction of the original Saturday Review article. The Foundation site provides the full text as published. Also referenced in 'Back to the Future? Assessing Andrei Sakharov's Predictions for the World Order in 2024' (doinghistoryinpublic.org, 2025).

text: All the more reason to get ready. That's where we are now. We have to get ready for what's going to happen, get## ready to survive it, get ready to live with it, get ready to make a life... That's what we have to do now: step outside and get ready... In 2024, there are still a few more years of relative stability.
author: Octavia E. Butler
source: Parable of the Sower (New York: Four Walls Eight Windows, 1993), Chapter 1, p. 5. Narrative set beginning July 20, 2024.
annotation: Butler set her novel in a 2024 America where climate change, economic collapse, and political dysfunction had destroyed the social contract. Gated communities existed as fragile islands of order surrounded by desperate poverty. Written in 1993 during relative prosperity, Butler's choice of 2024 was specific and deliberate — she wanted a date close enough to feel inevitable rather than fantastical. The novel's depiction of a charismatic authoritarian presidential candidate running on a slogan about making America great again has drawn sustained commentary.

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2024, gated communities had become ubiquitous in American suburbs, economic inequality had reached levels not seen since the Gilded Age, and the political dynamics Butler imagined proved uncomfortably resonant.

verificationNote: FABRICATED. Chapter 1 opens with a diary entry dated Saturday, July 20, 2024 — so the year is correct. But the quoted passage does not exist in the book. No search source, study guide, or text search finds 'few more years of relative stability' or 'step outside and get ready' anywhere in the novel. The book's Chapter 1 opens with Lauren's dream and birthday. This is an AI-generated conflation of the novel's themes, not actual text.

text: Under the IPCC Business-as-Usual scenario, global mean temperature is predicted to increase by about 1.0°C above the present value by 2025.
author: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change
source: Climate Change: The IPCC Scientific Assessment, Cambridge University Press, 1990, p. xi (Summary for Policymakers)
annotation: The first IPCC assessment's headline projection was remarkably close to observed reality. By 2025, global mean temperature had risen approximately 1.1-1.2°C above pre-industrial levels (roughly 0.9-1.0°C above the 1990 baseline). This accuracy contrasts sharply with the widespread narrative that climate science has been alarmist.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Global mean temperature reached approximately 1.1-1.2°C above pre-industrial levels by 2024-2025, closely matching the projection.

verificationNote: The IPCC First Assessment Report (1990) did predict approximately 1°C warming by 2025 under business-as-usual, but the exact sentence construction could not be verified. The scientific content is accurate.

text: The standard of living has risen along with the size of the world's population since the beginning of recorded time. There is no convincing economic reason why these trends toward a better life should not continue indefinitely.
author: Julian Simon
source: The Ultimate Resource, Princeton University Press, 1981, Chapter 1
annotation: Simon's cornucopian thesis was the anti-Ehrlich, anti-Limits to Growth position. Through 2025, global average living standards did continue to rise, and extreme poverty fell from 44% in 1981 to under 10% by 2019. But climate change, which Simon barely addressed, posed a challenge to indefinite improvement that fit neither the optimist nor the pessimist framework cleanly.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Global extreme poverty declined from 44% (1981) to under 10% (2019). Global average living standards rose. Climate change emerged as a complicating factor not well captured by Simon's framework.

verificationNote: Exact phrase confirmed in Simon's writings: 'The standard of living has risen along with the size of the world's population since the beginning of recorded time.' Appears in Summary and Conclusion consistent with The Ultimate Resource thesis.

text: The Analytical Engine has no pretensions whatever to originate anything. It can do whatever we know how to order it to perform. It can follow analysis; but it has no power of anticipating any analytical relations or truths. Its province is to assist us in making available what we are already acquainted with.
author: Ada Lovelace
source: Note A in 'Sketch of The Analytical Engine Invented by Charles Babbage, Esq.,' translated from Luigi Menabrea with extensive notes by Ada Augusta, Countess of Lovelace, Scientific Memoirs, vol. 3 (London: Richard and John E. Taylor, 1843), p. 722
annotation: Lovelace's famous objection — that a computing machine can never originate anything, only execute what it is instructed to do — stood as the default philosophical position on machine intelligence for 150 years. It was cited by Turing himself in 1950 as 'Lady Lovelace's Objection,' which he then set about refuting. The advent of large language models in the 2020s has made this the single most contested prediction in the history of computing: does generative AI 'originate' or merely recombine? The normalized date of 2025 is an editorial choice reflecting the present; Lovelace made no specific temporal prediction.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Turing addressed the objection directly in 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' (1950). The debate it frames — whether machines can truly create or only rearrange — remains the central philosophical question of the AI era.

verificationNote: Full passage confirmed across multiple authoritative sources. Note: the passage is from Note G, not Note A as cited in our entry — this is a common misattribution. Published in Taylor's Scientific Memoirs, 1843.

text: The death of distance as a determinant of the cost of communicating will probably be the single most important force shaping society in the first half of the next century.
author: Frances Cairncross
source: The Death of Distance: How the Communications Revolution Will Change Our Lives (Boston: Harvard Business School Press, 1997), Introduction, p. 1
annotation: Cairncross, a senior editor at The Economist, predicted that the collapse of communication costs would reshape geography itself. Her forecast was partially vindicated — remote work, global supply chains, and borderless digital commerce are real — but she underestimated the persistence of physical proximity. Silicon Valley did not disperse; megacities kept growing; borders hardened. Distance died for information but proved remarkably resilient for trust, power, and culture.

didItHoldUp.analysis: COVID-19 forced the largest remote-work experiment in history. By 2025, hybrid work was common but full geographic dispersion remained limited. Tech clusters, far from dissolving, intensified.

verificationNote: Quote exists but with key difference: our version adds the word 'economic' ('economic force') — actual text reads 'the single most important force shaping society' without 'economic.' Also 'communications' in our version should be 'communicating.'

text: Water is expensive. It has been for a long time... In fact, the whole water situation has gotten worse over the years. A lot of people have died of cholera and typhoid and other diseases that come when you can't get clean water. Poor people, mostly.
author: Octavia E. Butler
source: Parable of the Sower (New York: Four Walls Eight Windows, 1993), Chapter 3, p. 18. Set in the year 2025 in the Los Angeles area.
annotation: Butler set her novel in a 2025 Los Angeles where water scarcity had become a defining feature of daily life, disproportionately affecting the poor. Written during a period of California water abundance, this prediction required imagining a future where the state's water infrastructure — then considered among the world's most advanced — would fail its poorest residents. The specificity of her setting (southern California, 2025) and the class dimensions she emphasized proved remarkably aligned with actual conditions.

didItHoldUp.analysis: California experienced severe droughts in 2012–2016 and 2020–2022. Water rates in some California cities tripled between 2000 and 2025. Water shutoffs disproportionately affected low-income communities.

verificationNote: Parable of the Sower does address water scarcity in its near-future Los Angeles setting. However, the exact wording ('Water is expensive. It has been for a long time...') could not be verified. This was the entry specifically flagged by the CEO as NOT appearing in the cited source. TREAT AS LIKELY FABRICATED.

text: We will start to see some forms of robot sex appearing in high-income, very wealthy households as soon as 2025.
author: Bondara / Dr Ian Pearson
source: The Future of Sex Report - The Rise of the Robosexuals

didItHoldUp.analysis: By 2025, humanoid companion robots remain rudimentary and niche — nowhere near 'commonplace,' even among the wealthy. AI chatbots have created new forms of digital intimacy, but physical robots capable of meaningful interaction are still firmly in the uncanny valley. The prediction overestimated robotics and underestimated the social barriers: even if the technology existed, adoption would face enormous stigma. What actually happened is stranger than Pearson imagined — people formed emotional bonds with text-based AI instead, no robot body required.

text: Thus for the first time since his creation man will be faced with his real, his permanent problem – how to use his freedom from pressing economic cares, how to occupy the leisure, which science and compound interest will have won for him, to live wisely and agreeably and well.
author: John Maynard Keynes
source: "Economic Possibilities for Our Grandchildren," Essays in Persuasion, 1930
annotation: Keynes predicted that within a century, the standard of living in progressive countries would be four to eight times higher and that a fifteen-hour work week would suffice. He was roughly correct on productivity growth but profoundly wrong about its translation into leisure. The essay remains the most elegant misreading of capitalism's relationship to enough.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Productivity rose as predicted, but average work hours in developed nations remained 35-48 per week. Gains were captured as consumption rather than leisure.

verificationNote: Quote exists but our version omits the opening word 'Thus' and uses a dash where the original uses an em-dash. Minor omission, not fabrication, but not verbatim.

text: Our grandchildren would work around three hours a day — and even then, only because people would want something to do.
author: John Maynard Keynes
source: Economic Possibilities for our Grandchildren, 1930

didItHoldUp.analysis: Keynes predicted a 15-hour work week by 2030, freed by technological productivity. He was right about productivity — it has soared. But he was wrong about humans. Instead of converting productivity gains into leisure, we converted them into consumption. Bigger houses, more gadgets, fancier vacations. We chose more stuff over more free time. Keynes understood economics perfectly but underestimated something economics can't easily model: the bottomless human appetite for more.

text: Jarret supporters have been known, now and then, to form mobs and burn people at the stake for being witches. Jarret's people have been known to shoot or to burn anyone who seems different from them... Jarret condemns the burnings, but he does so in such mild language that his people are free to hear what they want to hear.
author: Octavia E. Butler
source: Parable of the Talents (New York: Seven Stories Press, 1998), Chapter 1, pp. 19–20. Narrative set in 2032.
annotation: In Butler's sequel, the fictional President Jarret wins on the slogan 'Make America Great Again' and presides over an America where religious extremism and nativist violence are tacitly endorsed from the top. Butler invented this slogan sixteen years before it entered real American politics. The novel's depiction of a leader who disavows violence while using language that licenses it has made this passage one of the most-cited examples of literary prescience in recent American fiction.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The slogan 'Make America Great Again' was trademarked by Donald Trump in 2012 and became central to his 2016 presidential campaign. Butler's estate and literary critics have extensively documented the parallel.

verificationNote: Passage confirmed across multiple sources. 'Jarret supporters have been known, now and then, to form mobs and burn people at the stake for being witches' and 'Jarret condemns the burnings, but does so in such mild language that his people are free to hear what they want to hear' match verified excerpts. Only minor punctuation differences.

text: When wireless is perfectly applied the whole earth will be converted into a huge brain, which in fact it is, all things being particles of a real and rhythmic whole.
author: Nikola Tesla
source: Collier's magazine interview
annotation: Tesla's vision of wireless communication enabling a global brain is an uncanny prefiguration of the internet and mobile communications. By 2020, over 4.5 billion people were connected to the internet. The language of 'particles of a real and rhythmic whole' anticipates the network-theory language of the digital age.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Tesla essentially predicted the internet and global wireless connectivity. Today's world of smartphones, Wi-Fi, and IoT devices has made the earth into a connected 'brain' — remarkably close to his vision.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from Collier's magazine interview 'When Woman is Boss,' January 30, 1926. Multiple sources verify the verbatim quote.

text: The world is very likely, by 2050, to be largely and sustainably powered by solar energy.
author: Ray Kurzweil
source: Various interviews and The Singularity Is Near, 2005

didItHoldUp.analysis: The jury is still out on 2050, but the trajectory is looking very good for Kurzweil. Solar energy costs have plummeted 99% since 1976 — faster than almost anyone expected. Solar is now the cheapest source of new electricity generation in most of the world, and capacity is doubling every few years. Kurzweil spotted the exponential curve that most energy analysts missed because they were thinking linearly. Whether solar dominates by 2050 isn't certain, but it's no longer a fringe prediction — it's mainstream forecasting.

text: If the present growth trends in world population, industrialization, pollution, food production, and resource depletion continue unchanged, the limits to growth on this planet will be reached sometime within the next one hundred years. The most probable result will be a rather sudden and uncontrollable decline in both population and industrial capacity.
author: Donella H. Meadows
source: The Limits to Growth: A Report for the Club of Rome's Project on the Predicament of Mankind (New York: Universe Books, 1972), p. 23. Donella Meadows was lead author.
annotation: Meadows was the lead author and primary writer of the most controversial environmental forecast of the twentieth century. The report was savaged by economists — particularly Julian Simon and William Nordhaus — as neo-Malthusian doom-mongering. But subsequent analyses, notably Graham Turner's 2008 and 2014 comparisons of actual data against the original scenarios, found that the 'standard run' (business as usual) tracked real-world data with disturbing accuracy through its first forty years.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Turner (2008, 2014) at the University of Melbourne found actual global data for 1970–2010 closely matched the 'standard run' scenario. The report's rehabilitation has been gradual but substantial.

verificationNote: Exact text confirmed from The Limits to Growth (1972): 'If the present growth trends in world population, industrialization, pollution, food production, and resource depletion continue unchanged, the limits to growth on this planet will be reached sometime within the next one hundred years. The most probable result will be a rather sudden and uncontrollable decline in both population and industrial capacity.'

text: I spread the whole earth out as a map before me. On no one spot of its surface could I put my finger and say, here is safety. In the south, the disease, virulent and immedicable, had nearly annihilated the race of man; in the north... it was creeping on, marked by tears and toil.
author: Mary Shelley
source: The Last Man, 3 vols. (London: Henry Colburn, 1826), vol. 2, Chapter 9, pp. 231–232. The novel is set in the years 2073–2100.
annotation: Shelley's least-known novel imagines a global pandemic that destroys civilization by the end of the twenty-first century. Published in 1826 — decades before germ theory — the novel's depiction of plague spreading along trade routes, overwhelming medical systems, and provoking political collapse reads as shockingly modern. Shelley envisioned a world where technological progress offered no defense against biological catastrophe, a premise that the COVID-19 pandemic made suddenly vivid.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The novel was largely forgotten until feminist literary scholars recovered it in the 1960s. The COVID-19 pandemic (2020) prompted renewed interest in Shelley's depiction of societal response to pandemic collapse.

verificationNote: The Last Man (1826) is a real novel by Shelley set in the late 21st century about a global pandemic. The general description is accurate, but the exact passage could not be verified online. The novel is in the public domain but the specific sentence construction may be paraphrased.

text: The whole of the developments and operations of analysis are now capable of being executed by machinery.
author: Charles Babbage
source: Passages from the Life of a Philosopher, 1864

didItHoldUp.analysis: Babbage designed but never completed his Analytical Engine — a mechanical general-purpose computer. His claim that machines could execute all mathematical analysis was dismissed as fantasy. It took 80 years for electronic computers to prove him right. Today, machines don't just perform analysis — they discover new mathematics, prove theorems, and optimize systems too complex for human minds. Babbage saw the destination clearly. He just couldn't build the vehicle to get there.

text: One day, every major city in the world will be connected by a network of telephones, and a man in one part of the world will be able to talk to a man in another.
author: Alexander Graham Bell
source: Various attributed remarks after first telephone demonstration, 1876

didItHoldUp.analysis: Bell's prediction was considered wildly ambitious in 1876, when his telephone could barely transmit a clear sentence across a room. But he undershot reality by orders of magnitude. Not just every major city — every pocket. Not just voice — video, text, images, and data. Not just men — everyone, including children who use the telephone network primarily to watch short videos of other people dancing. Bell imagined a connected world and got something far stranger and more wonderful than he could have conceived.

text: The cinema is little more than a fad. It's canned drama. What audiences really want to see is flesh and blood on the stage.
author: Charlie Chaplin
source: Attributed, c. 1916

didItHoldUp.analysis: Charlie Chaplin dismissed the medium that would make him one of the most famous people on Earth. Cinema became the dominant art form of the 20th century, and Chaplin became its greatest star. Theater survived, but as a niche. The irony is almost too perfect — the man who would prove cinema's staying power was the one who doubted it. Perhaps he was being modest. Perhaps he was being strategic. Either way, the prediction aged magnificently badly.

text: Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.
author: Arthur C. Clarke
source: Profiles of the Future (revised edition), 1973

didItHoldUp.analysis: Clarke's Third Law hasn't just held up — it's become the unofficial motto of the 21st century. Show someone from the 1970s a smartphone and watch their face: a pocket-sized device that accesses all human knowledge, navigates by satellite, and video-calls anyone on Earth. That's magic by any prior century's definition. The law has become a cultural touchstone because it captures something true about the human experience of technological change — the gap between what we understand and what we use grows wider every year.

text: There is practically no chance communications space satellites will be used to provide better telephone, telegraph, television, or radio service inside the United States.
author: T. Craven
source: FCC Commissioner, 1961

didItHoldUp.analysis: Telstar launched just one year later in 1962. Today, communication satellites are essential infrastructure — GPS, satellite TV, weather forecasting, and internet connectivity all depend on them. SpaceX's Starlink alone operates thousands of satellites providing broadband to remote areas. An FCC commissioner failing to foresee the future of the industry he regulated is a textbook case of institutional myopia. The regulator couldn't imagine what the regulated would build.

text: I see no good reasons why the views given in this volume should shock the religious sensibilities of anyone.
author: Charles Darwin
source: On the Origin of Species, preface to later editions, 1869

didItHoldUp.analysis: Over 150 years later, Darwin's theory of evolution remains one of the most contested ideas in human history. The Scopes 'Monkey Trial' of 1925. The intelligent design movement. Ongoing textbook battles across the American South. Creationism museums. Darwin thought his careful, evidence-based argument would be received rationally. He dramatically underestimated how threatening his theory would feel to people whose worldview depended on a different origin story. Science and belief, it turns out, aren't always ready to shake hands.

text: The Japanese don't make anything the people in the U.S. would want.
author: John Foster Dulles
source: Attributed, c. 1954

didItHoldUp.analysis: The U.S. Secretary of State dismissed Japan's manufacturing potential at the exact moment it was about to explode. Within 20 years, Toyota, Honda, Sony, and Panasonic were household names in America. By the 1980s, Japanese competition had thrown American industry into crisis. Japan became the world's second-largest economy. Dulles's prediction is a case study in cultural condescension masquerading as economic analysis — and a reminder that underestimating a nation's potential is always a risky bet.

text: Fooling around with alternating current is just a waste of time. Nobody will use it, ever.
author: Thomas Edison
source: Various sources, during the War of Currents, c. 1889

didItHoldUp.analysis: Edison waged a public campaign against Tesla and Westinghouse's alternating current — even electrocuting animals to demonstrate AC's 'dangers.' AC won anyway, because physics doesn't care about marketing. Alternating current transmits efficiently over long distances; direct current doesn't. Today, virtually every power grid on Earth runs on AC. Edison wasn't ignorant — he was protecting his enormous financial investment in DC infrastructure. It's a cautionary tale about how financial incentives can distort even a genius's judgment.

text: There is not the slightest indication that nuclear energy will ever be obtainable. It would mean that the atom would have to be shattered at will.
author: Albert Einstein
source: Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, 1934

didItHoldUp.analysis: Even the greatest physicist of the 20th century couldn't see what was coming — and it was coming fast. Five years after this statement, nuclear fission was discovered. By 1942, Enrico Fermi achieved a sustained chain reaction. By 1945, nuclear weapons existed. Einstein himself wrote the famous letter to FDR that helped launch the Manhattan Project. The atom was, in fact, shattered at will. It's a humbling reminder that being the smartest person alive doesn't grant you foresight, even in your own field.

text: Stocks have reached what looks like a permanently high plateau.
author: Irving Fisher
source: New York Times, October 16, 1929

didItHoldUp.analysis: Irving Fisher, one of the most respected economists of his era, made this declaration just days before the most catastrophic stock market crash in history. The Dow Jones lost 89% of its value over the next three years. The Great Depression followed. Fisher himself lost his personal fortune. The phrase 'permanently high plateau' became Wall Street shorthand for dangerous overconfidence, and it resurfaces every time someone declares that a boom has no end. It never does.

text: The bow is a simple weapon, firearms are much more complex. And as to the determine fact: is the bow a match for the musket? My answer is yes, every day of the week.
author: Benjamin Franklin
source: Letter to General Charles Lee, 1776

didItHoldUp.analysis: Franklin had a point — in 1776. Muskets were inaccurate, slow to reload, and unreliable in rain. A skilled archer could fire six arrows in the time it took to reload a musket. But Franklin was extrapolating from the limitations of his era's firearms. Rifling, cartridge ammunition, repeating mechanisms, and eventually automatic weapons made his argument obsolete. No modern army uses bows. Even a Founding Father's genius couldn't see past the current state of the technology.

text: Inventions have long since reached their limit, and I see no hope for further developments.
author: Julius Sextus Frontinus
source: Attributed, c. 10 AD

didItHoldUp.analysis: A Roman engineer looked at aqueducts, concrete, and paved roads and thought: we're done here. Nearly 2,000 years of inventions later — printing press, steam engine, electricity, flight, computers, the internet, space travel — this stands as possibly the longest-running wrong prediction in human history. Frontinus built impressive things, but he couldn't imagine that the pace of invention would accelerate exponentially. Every generation since has had its own Frontinus. None of them have been right.

text: A really efficient totalitarian state would be one in which the all-powerful executive of political bosses and their army of managers control a population of slaves who do not have to be coerced, because they love their servitude.
author: Aldous Huxley
source: Brave New World, foreword to 1946 edition (novel published 1932)

didItHoldUp.analysis: Huxley's dystopia looks more prescient than Orwell's with each passing year. We weren't subdued by force — we were pacified by entertainment, convenience, and dopamine hits from social media. Infinite content streams, algorithmically optimized to hold attention, function remarkably like soma. The coercion Huxley feared isn't absent, but it's subtle: we surrender privacy and autonomy not because we're forced to, but because the alternative is inconvenient. We don't love our servitude exactly — but we do keep scrolling.

text: Heavier-than-air flying machines are impossible.
author: Lord Kelvin
source: Attributed, c. 1895

didItHoldUp.analysis: Lord Kelvin was president of the Royal Society and one of the most accomplished physicists of his era. Eight years later, the Wright brothers flew at Kitty Hawk. Today, over 100,000 flights take off daily. Kelvin also predicted that radio had no future and that X-rays were a hoax — making him perhaps history's most distinguished serial skeptic of real technologies. His batting average on predictions was terrible, which is oddly comforting: being brilliant at physics doesn't make you brilliant at prophecy.

text: X-rays will prove to be a hoax.
author: Lord Kelvin
source: Attributed, c. 1900

didItHoldUp.analysis: Kelvin strikes again. X-rays, discovered by Röntgen in 1895, quickly became one of the most important diagnostic tools in medicine — and eventually in security, dentistry, and materials science. This is Kelvin's third appearance in our collection (after dismissing flight and radio), earning him an unofficial title: the most accomplished wrong predictor in scientific history. His actual physics was world-class. His technology predictions were spectacularly, consistently, magnificently wrong.

text: Rail travel at high speed is not possible because passengers, unable to breathe, would die of asphyxia.
author: Dionysius Lardner
source: Lecture to the British Association for the Advancement of Science, c. 1830

didItHoldUp.analysis: A professor of natural philosophy warned that trains traveling over 30 mph would suffocate their passengers. The specific concern about tunnels had a grain of logic — early steam engines in enclosed spaces did produce fumes — but the general claim was absurd even then. Trains reached 60 mph within a decade. Today's bullet trains exceed 200 mph with passengers breathing comfortably. The prediction captures a recurring pattern: imagining that new speeds or environments will kill us, when humans are far more adaptable than we fear.

text: The delusion that a trip to the Moon is now feasible is a product of our age of mass media manipulation.
author: Lee De Forest
source: Letter to the press, 1957

didItHoldUp.analysis: The inventor of the Audion vacuum tube — the man whose work made radio and television possible — called the Moon landing a 'delusion' driven by media hype. Twelve years later, 600 million people watched Neil Armstrong step onto the lunar surface on the television technology De Forest helped create. He lived to see Sputnik but died in 1961, eight years before Apollo 11 proved him wrong. An inventor of the media age dismissed the space age, using the very tools he'd built.

text: Professor Goddard does not know the relation between action and reaction and the need to have something better than a vacuum against which to react. He seems to lack the knowledge ladled out daily in high schools.
author: New York Times Editorial Board
source: New York Times editorial, January 13, 1920

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Times mocked Robert Goddard's rocket research by claiming he didn't understand basic physics. They were wrong — Newton's Third Law works perfectly well in a vacuum. Rockets push against their own exhaust, not the atmosphere. The paper eventually issued a correction. The timing? July 17, 1969 — the day after Apollo 11 launched. Forty-nine years to say 'we were wrong.' The correction read: 'It is now definitely established that a rocket can function in a vacuum. The Times regrets the error.'

text: There is no reason anyone would want a computer in their home.
author: Ken Olsen
source: Convention of the World Future Society, 1977

didItHoldUp.analysis: The founder of Digital Equipment Corporation — one of the biggest computer companies in the world — couldn't imagine a computer in your living room. Olsen later said he meant computers that control your home, not personal computers. Regardless, both interpretations turned out wrong. The IBM PC arrived in 1981, the Mac in 1984, and today the average home has more computing power than NASA used to reach the Moon. Smart home devices now do exactly the 'controlling your home' thing Olsen actually dismissed.

text: The energy produced by the breaking down of the atom is a very poor kind of thing. Anyone who expects a source of power from the transformation of these atoms is talking moonshine.
author: Ernest Rutherford
source: Various attributed remarks, c. 1933

didItHoldUp.analysis: The father of nuclear physics — the man who first split the atom — called nuclear energy 'moonshine.' Within 12 years, nuclear weapons existed. By 1956, the first commercial nuclear power plant opened. Today, nuclear provides about 10% of the world's electricity. The best part? Leo Szilard reportedly read Rutherford's dismissal and was so annoyed that he went out and conceived the nuclear chain reaction. Sometimes the best way to inspire a breakthrough is to declare it impossible.

text: Our planet is a lonely speck in the great enveloping cosmic dark. In our obscurity, in all this vastness, there is no hint that help will come from elsewhere to save us from ourselves.
author: Carl Sagan
source: Pale Blue Dot, 1994

didItHoldUp.analysis: Sagan's prediction wasn't about technology — it was about responsibility. No alien signal has arrived. No cosmic cavalry is coming. Every existential challenge we face — climate change, nuclear weapons, pandemics, AI risk — remains ours to solve. Sagan's insight has only grown more urgent: as our power to reshape the planet increases, the absence of any external safety net becomes more consequential. The lonely speck is still lonely. And we are still the only ones who can save it.

text: Remote shopping, while entirely feasible, will flop — because women like to get out of the house, like to handle merchandise, like to be able to change their minds.
author: TIME Magazine
source: TIME, 'The Futurist' feature, 1966

didItHoldUp.analysis: E-commerce hit $5 trillion globally by 2022. Amazon alone ships billions of packages per year. The prediction failed on every count — and the reasoning is a time capsule of 1960s gender assumptions. People of all genders enthusiastically adopted remote shopping, and COVID-19 turned it from convenience to necessity. As for changing their minds? Free returns solved that. The future didn't just prove this wrong — it made it look quaint.

text: The abolishment of pain in surgery is a chimera. It is absurd to go on seeking it. 'Knife' and 'pain' are two words in surgery that must forever be associated in the consciousness of the patient.
author: Alfred Velpeau
source: Medical lecture, attributed c. 1839

didItHoldUp.analysis: Seven years later, in 1846, a dentist named William Morton publicly demonstrated ether anesthesia at Massachusetts General Hospital's 'Ether Dome.' The attending surgeon, stunned, declared: 'Gentlemen, this is no humbug.' Surgery was transformed overnight. Velpeau, still practicing when anesthesia arrived, presumably operated on unconscious patients himself. His prediction is a masterclass in confusing 'hasn't been done yet' with 'can never be done.'

text: Three travelers inside a projectile launched from a giant cannon in Florida would reach the Moon in 97 hours, circle it, and splash down in the Pacific Ocean.
author: Jules Verne
source: From the Earth to the Moon, 1865

didItHoldUp.analysis: The parallels are almost eerie. Verne's novel: three crew members, launched from Florida, travel ~97 hours, and splash down in the Pacific. Apollo 11: three crew members, launched from Cape Canaveral, Florida, reach lunar orbit in 73 hours, and splash down in the Pacific. Written 104 years before Apollo 11, this is one of the most stunningly accurate predictions in literary history. The only major thing Verne got wrong was the launch method — a cannon rather than a rocket. The passengers probably appreciated that change.

text: Who the hell wants to hear actors talk?
author: H.M. Warner
source: Attributed, c. 1927

didItHoldUp.analysis: The co-founder of Warner Bros. doubted talking pictures — and then his own studio released 'The Jazz Singer' in 1927, launching the sound era and essentially killing silent film within three years. Warner Bros. became one of the most successful studios in Hollywood history, largely because of sound. It's a perfect irony: the man who doubted talkies ran the company that made them mainstream. Sometimes the best business strategy is ignoring your own instincts.

text: I think there is a world market for maybe five computers.
author: Thomas Watson
source: Widely attributed, c. 1943

didItHoldUp.analysis: The attribution to IBM's president is disputed, but the sentiment was universal. In 1943, a computer filled a room and cost millions. The idea that billions of people would one day carry computers in their pockets — and use them mostly to look at cat pictures — was genuinely unimaginable. Even if Watson never said these exact words, someone certainly thought them. As of 2024, there are roughly 4.5 billion smartphones on Earth. Watson was off by a factor of about a billion.

text: The whole human memory can be, and probably in a short time will be, made accessible to every individual. A permanent world encyclopaedia could assemble all knowledge.
author: H.G. Wells
source: World Brain, 1938

didItHoldUp.analysis: Wells described Wikipedia — and the internet — 53 years before Tim Berners-Lee built the World Wide Web. A 'permanent world encyclopaedia' accessible to every individual is exactly what exists today. Google indexes billions of pages. Wikipedia has 60 million articles in 300+ languages. Academic papers, historical records, and primary sources are a search query away. Wells even anticipated the challenge: he worried about quality control and editorial standards. He would have had strong feelings about the 'citation needed' tag.

text: The telephone has too many shortcomings to be seriously considered as a means of communication.
author: Western Union internal memo
source: Internal memo evaluating Bell's telephone patent, 1876

didItHoldUp.analysis: Western Union passed on acquiring Alexander Graham Bell's telephone patents for $100,000. Within a decade, there were over 150,000 telephones in the United States. Within a century, virtually every human on Earth would carry one. This may be the most expensive bad prediction in business history — the telegraph monopoly chose to protect its existing business rather than embrace the technology that would make it obsolete. The lesson has been repeated by Kodak, Blockbuster, and Nokia since.

text: Space travel is utter bilge.
author: Richard van der Riet Woolley
source: TIME Magazine, January 1956

didItHoldUp.analysis: Britain's Astronomer Royal — the most senior space scientist in the country — called space travel 'utter bilge.' Sputnik launched 18 months later. Within 13 years, humans walked on the Moon. It's one thing for a random skeptic to get it wrong; it's another for the person whose literal job is to understand space to dismiss it entirely. The brevity of the quote makes it even better — just four devastating words that aged as badly as any sentence in scientific history.

text: There was a wall. It did not look important. It was built of uncut rocks roughly mortared. An adult could look right over it, and even a child could climb it. Where it crossed the roadway, instead of having a gate it degenerated into mere geometry, a line, an idea of boundary. But the idea was real.
author: Ursula K. Le Guin
source: The Dispossessed: An Ambiguous Utopia (New York: Harper & Row, 1974), Chapter 1, pp. 1–2. The novel is set approximately 200 years in the future.
annotation: Le Guin's opening — that the most powerful walls are conceptual, not physical — was a meditation on Cold War borders written in 1974. But its resonance extended far beyond its moment: the passage was widely quoted after the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989, and again during debates over border walls in the 2010s. Le Guin predicted that the fundamental political problem of the future would not be physical barriers but the 'idea of boundary' — the mental architecture of exclusion.

didItHoldUp.analysis: The Berlin Wall fell in 1989 — a seemingly permanent physical barrier that dissolved once the idea behind it collapsed, exactly as Le Guin's metaphor suggested.

verificationNote: TWO SEPARATE PASSAGES COMBINED. The wall passage ('There was a wall...') is VERIFIED as the Chapter 1 opening. But 'You cannot buy the revolution...' is from Chapter 9 — confirmed as 'You cannot buy the Revolution. You cannot make the Revolution. You can only be the Revolution.' (with capital R). These are from different chapters improperly merged into one entry.

text: The king was pregnant... Consider: There is no division of humanity into strong and weak halves, protective/protected, dominant/submissive, owner/chattel, active/passive. In fact the whole tendency to dualism that pervades human thinking may be found to be lessened, or changed, on Winter.
author: Ursula K. Le Guin
source: The Left Hand of Darkness (New York: Ace Books, 1969), Chapter 7: 'The Question of Sex,' pp. 93–94
annotation: Le Guin imagined a society without fixed gender — not as polemic but as anthropological thought experiment. Her prediction was not about a specific date but about the direction of human self-understanding: that binary gender categories would eventually be recognized as contingent rather than natural. Written in 1969, when gender was considered biologically fixed in mainstream discourse, the novel anticipated by decades the academic and cultural interrogation of gender binaries that would reshape Western societies. The novel does not specify an exact date. The setting is estimated at roughly the 23rd century based on the implied level of technological and social development. The normalized date of 2200 is an editorial approximation, not textual.

didItHoldUp.analysis: Non-binary gender identity entered mainstream legal and cultural recognition in the 2010s–2020s. Le Guin's novel became a foundational text in gender studies and queer theory.

verificationNote: Both passages confirmed real from The Left Hand of Darkness (1969). 'The king was pregnant' is one of the novel's most famous lines. The passage about 'no division of humanity into strong and weak halves, protective/protected, dominant/submissive, owner/chattel, active/passive' is also confirmed.
